ARB 410
Research methods in literature
3 credits
TOTAL: 42 Credits
Course Descriptions
ARB 300: Styles of Literary Expression, 3 credits
This course is intended to acquaint students with the various genres of Arabic literature, ancient and modern (poetry, prose, the essay, fiction, biography, drama) and with certain grammatical and linguistic studies to familiarize them with the various modes of literary expression in Arabic.
Students will practice writing used in various genres.
ARB 310: Introduction to Syntax aad Morphology, 3 credits
This course deals with studying the Arabic word structure regarding its derivation, affixation, vocalization, etc... It studies, as well, the derivative types, the system of the Arabic nominal sentence and its nuclear constituents, and the different types of both the subject and predicate and their relatings; such as anastrophe and the annullers, with a special attention to applications of these issues to the Qur'anic and different literary texts, and analyzing these texts in the light of modern linguistic methods.
ARB 320: Arabic Rhetoric (1), 3 credits
This course is intended to acquaint students with the rhetorical heritage of Arabic as represented by the three disciplines "al-Ma'ani" (Semantics), "al-Bayan" (Eloquence), and "al-Badi" (Art of Metaphors). The course also aims at directing the students' attention to the style of the Holy Qur'an and the aesthetic value of great literary texts.
ARB 330: Literary Texts Analysis, 3 credits
This course deals with analyzing a variety of literary texts belonging to several genres from different periods. It aims at acquainting the students with the literary and linguistic analyses in the light of the old and modern analytical methods in language and criticism.
ARB 341: Pre-Islamic Literature, 3 credits
This course explores pre-Islamic poetry, its beginnings, and the influence of the Bedouin and urban environments on it. The course discusses matters like narration, recording, plagiarism, and documentation. The course also studies themes and artistic characteristics of poetry and surveys the achievements of pre-Islamic poets and their poetic tendencies, with examples chosen particularly from outcasts and warriors. Additionally, the course studies examples from pre-Islamic prose in an attempt to understand its artistic qualities.
ARB 342: Early Islamic and Umayyad Literature, 3 credits
This course surveys the literature of the early Islamic period and that of the Umayyads. It discusses the major issues, such as the attitude of Islam towards poetry, texts, and authors, linking them to the historical background. The course also studies the influences exerted by the Holy Qur'an and Hadith on the various genres, and the new modes of expression that appeared under the orthodox caliphs and the Umayyads.
( Prerequisite: ARB 341)
ARB 350: General Linguistics, 3 credits
This course provides a survey of modem linguistic theories and their methodologies and applications to ancient and modem texts. It studies the concept of language, its nature and functions, and its psycho-socio-linguistic concepts.
ARB 351: Phonetics, 3 credits
This course introduces students to Arabic phonetics and explains its relation to other branches of linguistics. Students will be trained to pronounce Arabic sounds correctly, distinguish between sounds and functions, understand the causes of sound change, and have some knowledge of the latest developments in the field. The course touches on the sound system of Arabic and the relation between phonetics and phonology, together with the methods of research and the modern apparatus available to teach this science.
( Prerequisite: ARB 350)
ARB 352: Prosody, 3 credits
This course focuses on the meters of Arabic poetry, its beginnings, parts, changes, rhythm;, and rhymes. The course also clarifies the developments of poetic meters and rhythm in different ages (from the age of al-muwashah to the age of the modern, new verse), with various poetic practices.
ARB 361; Traditional Literary Criticism, 3 credits
This course is intended to acquaint students with the critical activity of the pre-Islamic, early Islamic, Umayyad, and Abbasids periods in order to familiarize them with its major theoreticians, trends, and issues through a study of the major critical texts.
ARB 362: Arabic Rhetoric (2), 3 credits
This course studies the system of the sentence in Arabic and its semantic characteristics, The Arabic rhetorical heritage where the student may be able to draw on its artistic and aesthetic values and apply the insights of this discipline to texts written in verse and prose selected from all periods, and comparing the achievement of the ancients with the modem critical and stylistic studies in order to combine originality with modernity.
(Prerequisite: ARB 320)
ARB 371: Abbasid Literature (1), 3 credits
This course surveys the history of poetry in the first Abbasid age and studies the achievements of the major poets of the period, linking them and their poetry to their cultural background. It also clarifies the intellectual, religious, and social phenomena that influenced their literary works.
ARB 372: Abbasid Literature (2), 3 credits
This course studies the major Arabic literary works in the late Abbasid era (4th and 5th century) as an expression of the spirit of the age. It focuses on intellectual and scientific developments and on literary issues in both poetry and prose.
(Prerequisite: ARB 371)
ARB 380: Syntax (2), 3 credits
This course tackles the Arabic verbal sentence regarding its nuclear constituents and the changes to which they submit; such as anastrophe, mentioning and deletion, with studying the sentence complements. It deals also with the prepositions and their meanings, the genitive, subordinators, and the major grammatical styles, with a special attention to applications to the Qur'anic and different literary texts, and analyzing these texts in the light of modern linguistic methods.
(Prerequisite: ARB 310)
ARB 382: Semantics and Arabic Lexicology, 3 credits
This course surveys ancient and modem questions connected with semantics and the various semantic theories that contribute to lexicography, as they are applied to Arabic dictionaries. The study of semantics touches on its theories. Lexicography, on the other hand, is treated as the application of semantics. In addition, the course considers the various schools of Arabic lexicography and their views on meaning and explanation.
(Prerequisite: ARB 350)
ARB 400: Research methods in language, 3 credits
This course focuses on acquainting the students with research in language regarding its concept, principles, characteristics, importance and sources and references. It introduces students to the methods of research in language; (historical, descriptive, normative and contrastive). Likewise, the course aims at helping students to apply these theoretical principles. Each student will be asked to write an independent research paper supervised by the instructor.
ARB 410: Research methods in literature, 3 credits
This course consists of two parts, the first of which is theoretical dealing with acquainting students with the concept of research, principles, characteristics, importance and sources. It deals, as well, with clarifying research methods in literature; such as the historical, psychological, social and other methods. The second part aims at training every student to write an independent research paper utilizing the above-mentioned theoretical knowledge.
ARB 411: Arabic Linguistics, 3 credits
This course focuses on the development of the linguistic structure of Arabic and the Arabic of the early Islamic era as described by the early Arab phoneticians. Attention should be paid to the various schools of thought among Arab philologists in the light of modern linguistic theory.
ARB 412: Andalusian and Maghribi Literature, 3 credits
This course surveys the literature of these areas and highlights its importance in the history of Arabic literature. It studies the literary relations between the eastern and western parts of the Arab world and stresses some of the new forms (musashshah and zajal) and themes (descriptions of nature, elegies on cities and states, the journey), and gives some attention to the most influential writers of verse and prose.
ARB 421: Late Medieval Literature, 3 credits
This course studies the Arabic literature of the new metropolitan areas, the Zinky's to the Ottoman era, and pays particular attention to the forms and patterns arising in the new environments: popular modes of expression reflecting the contemporary state of the Islamic world. Literary texts of different ages in both poetry and prose will be analyzed.
ARB 422: Arabic Library/Arabic Heritage, 3 credits
This course studies the history of writing and recording the great books in the traditional Arabic heritage. Students also become acquainted with main sources of literature such as the anthologies, collections of poetry, general literary books, biographies, and the great books in language,
criticism, syntax, rhetoric, lexicons, and encyclopedias.
ARB 431: Philology, 3 credits
This course surveys the philosophy of language among the ancients and the moderns and the effects of these philosophies on matters like homonymy and antonymy. The course also deals with the origins of human language and its development in childhood, and with phonetic, morphological, and grammatical systems.
ARB 432: Exercises in Syntax and Morphology, 3 credits
This course is intended to give practical training in matters covered in the theoretical courses in courses in grammar and morphology that the student has already studied. Its aim is to reinforce the students' theoretical and practical knowledge by helping them to acquire the necessary skills in oral and written expression. The texts chosen will be selected so as to instill in the students' minds the basic patterns of Arabic sentences, both nominal and verbal and to represent the different ages of Arabic language and literature.
(Prerequisite: ARB 380)
ARB 441: Modern Arabic Literature, 3 credits
This course covers the rise and development of modem literary genres written in verse and prose and studies some of the major figures and texts. It touches on the following topics with specific analytical readings in poetry, novels, stories, and plays: General influences on modern Arabic Literature, causes of development; poetry - movements and major figures; prose - forms and major figures.
ARB 442; Modern Literary Criticism, 3 credits
This course involves a study of the various modern critical theories and their application. The major representatives of each school will receive particular attention and the major issues of criticism will be discussed.
ARB 451: Readings in the Critical and Rhetorical Heritage, 3 credits
This course encourages students to learn to appreciate our critical and rhetorical heritage by familiarizing them with texts from the major references. Students will study the critical and rhetorical issues raised by these texts and the influence they have exercised on our modern critics. Students are thus expected to approve or disapprove in accordance with objective standards originating in the past but modified by modern endeavors.
ARB 452: Modern Arabic Gulf Literature, 3 credits
This course deals with contemporary Arabic literature in the Gulf: its relation to Arabic literature in general, its development, kinds, and trends, with an emphasis on the literature of the United Arab Emirates.
ARB 461: Stylistics, 3 credits
This course introduces students to stylistics and its contribution to modern language studies. The course surveys modern trends in the study of style and its relation to linguistics and criticism as well as the most important linguistic approaches to the study of style.
Major in English Literature
The English Literature Major exposes students to major authors, works, literary movements, and ways of thinking about the literature of England and the United States, as well as literature in the English language that has been written outside of these geographic borders. Students will acquire historical and critical contexts for evaluating literary works. Emphases on reading, analytic writing, and critical study are designed to satisfy both the needs of the undergraduate seeking a broad humanistic education and those of the scholar contemplating graduate study.
Graduates with a major in English Literature will be able to
• Read complex works of literature and criticism in English.
• Write informally and analytically in English.
• Understand the relation of literary works to their historical contexts and cultural surroundings.
• Consider the extent to which literature serves as a vehicle that connects individuals across boundaries of time and space.
Required Courses (9 credits)

ENG 300
Listening and Speaking (1)
3 credits
ENG 310
Writing (1)
3 credits
ENG 350
Basic Grammar and Usage
3 credits
Students entering the English Literature Major maybe exempted from any of the courses listed above by petitioning to take an exam and passing it at the level expected of graduates of each particular course. Students who do so should make up these credits by taking additional upper level literature courses.
Required Courses (12 credits)

ENG 320
Introduction to Literature
3 credits
ENG 330
Survey of British Literature
3 credits
ENG 340
Survey of American Literature
3 credits
ENG 400
Introduction to Criticism/Writing About Literature
3 credits
Required Course (3 credits)
Students must take one of the following two courses:

ENG 431
Elizabethan and 17th Century Literature
3 credits
ENG 432
Restoration and Enlightenment Literature
3 credits
Electives (9 credits chosen from among the following courses)

ENG 441
Romantic and Victorian Literature
3 credits
ENG 442
Twentieth-Century British Literature
3 credits
ENG 451
19th Century American Literature
3 credits
ENG 452
Twentieth-Century American Literature
3 credits
ENG 461
Modern World Literature
3 credits
ENG 462
Anglophone Literature outside of England and the United States
3 credits
Capstone Experience (6 credits)

ENG 410
Methods of Research
3 credits
ENG 420
Major Writer
3 credits
Total: 39 Credits
Course Descriptions
ENG 300: Listening and Speaking 1, 3 credits
This course introduces students to four main areas: (1) using correct grammatical structures in spoken English; (2) developing a speaking vocabulary useful for their academic work in the Department; (3) acquiring standard pronunciation useful for an international setting; and (4) developing listening and speaking strategies useful for effective communication.
ENG 310: Writing 1, 3 credits
This course is an introduction to the composition process from the sentence on to the paragraph, with emphasis on editing and revising for format, usage, punctuation, spelling, and capitalization. Sentence patterns and methods of sentence formation to convey a central idea are introduced and employed in the composition of descriptive, comparative, and explanatory paragraphs.
ENG 320: Introduction to Literature, 3 credits
This course introduces beginners to the three major genres of literature: poetry, fiction, and drama. It acquaints them with the basic concepts and terms with which to discuss literature. The course covers a wide selection of texts from all periods.
ENG 330: Survey of British Literature, 3 credits
This course is designed to introduce students to English literature from the Elizabethan period through the twentieth century and will focus on the development of various literary genres, as well as on the works of the most significant literary figures.
Prerequisite: ENG 320
ENG 340: Survey of American Literature, 3 credits
This course is designed to introduce students to American literature from the 17th through the twentieth centuries, focusing on major figures, differing literary genres, and shifting definitions of national identity.
Prerequisite: ENG 320
ENG 350: Basic Grammar and Usage, 3 credits
This course is designed to activate the beginning student's passive language base and Make him/her more conscious of the basic formal workings of grammar in English. Emphasis is on grammar use in oral and written communication.
ENG 400: Introduction to Criticism/Writing About Literature, 3 credits
This course introduces students to major issues in literary criticism and trains students in Practical criticism of specific literary texts. Students will read a variety of short literary works, as well as critical responses to these works, and will focus on writing critical essays of their own. Emphasis is on developing linguistic, critical, and analytic competencies.
Prerequisites: ENG 320 and one of the following: ENG 330 or ENG 340
ENG 410: Methods of Research, 3 credits
This course is intended to make students knowledgeable and effective at using the computer and library resources for producing a complete research paper. The focus of this course is also on other areas of professional writing, such as descriptive and analytical bibliography, note-taking, editing and rewriting of manuscripts, literature reviewing, and the ethics of documenting a paper.
Prerequisites: ENG 320, ENG 330, and ENG 340
ENG 420: Major British/American Writer, 3 credits
This course concentrates students' efforts on the work of one major British or American writer to be selected by the instructor each time the course is offered. The course is designed to enable students to do extensive research on a well-defined body of work and use that research to produce an extensive and original final paper.
Prerequisite: ENG 410
ENG 431: Elizabethan and 17th Century Literature, 3 credits
This course acquaints students with the various forms of literature produced by the major dramatists (e.g. Shakespeare), poets (particularly the Metaphysical poets), and prose writers (e.g. Sidney and Bacon) as well as with the cultural background of Elizabeth Literature.
Prerequisites: ENG 320 and ENG 330
ENG 432: Restoration and Enlightenment Literature, 3 credits
This course acquaints students with works by the major writers of the period from 1660 to 1800 and highlights major literary forms characteristic of the period such as the epic (Milton), the comedy of manners (Congreve), and satire (Swift, Pope).
Prerequisites: ENG 320 and ENG 330
ENG 441: Romantic and Victorian Literature, 3 credits
This course introduces students to the romantic Movement and the literature of the Victorian period, including poetry, fiction, and essays.
Prerequisite: ENG 320 and ENG, 330
ENG 442: 20th Century British Literature, 3 credits
This course examines key writers of this century, with an emphasis on thematic and stylistic analysis and with an attempt to understand the ways in which literature in the last century responded to critical cultural, economic and political forces.
Prerequisites: ENG 320 and ENG 330
ENG 451: 19th Century American Literature, 3 credits
This course provides students with a comprehensive survey of American literature from Washington Irving to the naturalists and realists at the turn of the century. It places the various movements and trends in their proper historical context.
Prerequisites: ENG 320 and ENG 340
ENG 452: Twentieth-Century American Literature, 3 credits
This course focuses on major writers whose texts appeared in the Twentieth Century and on the genres and movements within which they worked.
Prerequisites: ENG 320 and ENG 340
ENG 461: Modern World Literature, 3 credits
This course examines twentieth-century and contemporary literature originally produced in languages other than English. Students will have access to primary texts in English translations.
Prerequisites: ENG 320, ENG 330 and ENG 340
ENG 462: Anglophone Literature outside of England and the United States, 3 credits
This course examines twentieth-century and contemporary literature produced in such locations as Ireland, India, the Caribbean, Canada, Africa, by writers who are both participating in and reacting against the conventions and assumptions of English and American literature.
Prerequisites: ENG 320, ENG 330 and ENG 340
Major in History
The History major is divided into two concentrations: a History Concentration for majors interested in concentrating on historical analysis and writing, and History and Archaeology Concentration for students interested in pursuing archeology and a career in museum work or conservation.
History Concentration
The History Concentration is intended to provide students with a broad background in the historical trends which have shaped the modern world in general and the particular forces which have led to the development of contemporary society, culture and politics in the Islamic World and the United Arab Emirates. Students are expected to learn not only the basic facts of history, but also the contemporary methodologies historians use to reconstruct and interpret the past. The courses emphasize learning historical material, analyzing and interpreting historical sources, and developing historical arguments. Students are also introduced to major schools of historical interpretation. The major is capped with a seminar experience in which the students develop and research a historical topic of their choice.
After gaining a broad background in World History, historical methods, and the history of the Islamic World, students choose two electives from each of two broad areas: the History of Islam and the Arab peoples and the Modem and Contemporary World. They then take two courses in thematic and comparative history which address major themes in the social, cultural or economic history of society.
History students need to be able to work effectively in both English and Arabic, reading sources and secondary literature in both languages. Emphasis will vary with the subject matter, but it is expected that courses will generally include readings in primary sources and secondary literature written in both English and Arabic. Language of emphasis is suggested below (A refers to Arabic and E refers to English):
Programmatic Goals
Students majoring in the History Concentration will:
• have an understanding of the historical forces shaping the modern world
• be able to read, analyze, and interpret historical sources and arguments
• be able to formulate theses about historical trends
• be able to develop and answer questions for historical research.
• be able to read and analyze various genres of secondary historical literature
• demonstrate familiarity with major examples of historical theory and practice.
Required Courses (21 credits)

HIS 300
Methodology and Historiography (E,A)
3 credits
HIS 321
World History: Origins to 1500 (E)
3 credits
HIS 322
Modern World History (E)
3 credits
HIS 342
History of the Islamic World: Origins to 1500 (A)
3 credits
HIS 412
History of the UAE (A)
3 credits
HIS 421
Modern and Contemporary Arab History (A)
3 credits
HIS 471
Modern and Contemporary History of the Arabian Gulf (A)
3 credits
Islam and the Arab World (2 Electives - 6 credits)

HIS 332
Ancient History and Archaeology of the Arabian Peninsula (A)
3 credits
HIS 341
The Rise of Islam (A)
3 credits
HIS 351
History of AI-Andulas and AI-Maghrib during the Muslim Period (A)
3 credits
HIS 352
History of the Umayyad and Abbasid States (A)
3 credits
HIS 371
History of Islamic East during the Crusades (A)
3 credits
The Modern and Contemporary World (2 Electives - 6 credits)

HIS 411
History and Civilization of Medieval Europe (E)
3 credits
HIS 422
Modern and Contemporary History of Africa (E)
3 credits
HIS 431
Modern and Contemporary European History (E)
3 credits
HIS 432
Modern and Contemporary History of America (E)
3 credits
HIS 452
Modern and Contemporary History of Islamic World (E,A)
3 credits
HIS 462
Modern and Contemporary History of Asia (E)
3 credits
Thematic and Comparative History: 6 credits
These courses are developed by faculty on topics that address central historical themes, with an emphasis on Arabic/Islamic or another world society in comparative perspective. Such themes as women and the family; the history of cities and urbanism; oral and local history; environmental history; migration and immigration; trade, commerce and cultural exchange are examples of topics that might be selected. Some topics may subsequently become regular offerings, and some may be recommended for students in other majors. (For example. History of Cities and Urbanism for Urban Studies.)

HIS 450
Special Topics (1) (A)
3 credits
HIS 460
Special Topics (2) (E)
3 credits
Required Capstone (3 credits)

HIS 400
Research Project (E,A)
3 credits
TOTAL: 42 Credits
History and Archaeology Concentration
The History and Archaeology Concentration of the History Major is intended to provide students with a general background in ancient and modern history along with a solid grounding in archaeology, archaeological techniques, and museum studies. The Concentration is more oriented to Ancient History than the History Concentration and students are expected to pursue topics that will enhance their archaeological training. After gaining a broad background in World History and the history of the Islamic World, those students choosing the History and Archaeology Concentration are required to take a set of six courses (18 Units) that focus on archaeology and museum studies.
Programmatic Goals
Students majoring in Archaeology Concentration will:
• have a broad background in ancient and modern history, with a concentration on the Ancient history of the region
• be able to read, analyze, and interpret historical sources and arguments
• understand the techniques, practices, and uses of archaeology
• be able to undertake archaeological analysis of artifacts and form archaeological theses
• be knowledgeable about conservation, museums, and museum practices.
Required Courses (15 credits)

HIS 321
World History - Origins to 1500 AD (E)
3 credits
HIS 322
Modern and Contemporary World History (E)
3 credits
HIS 342
History of the Islamic World: Origins to 1500 AD (A)
3 credits
HIS 412
History of the United Arab Emirates (A)
3 credits
HIS 421
Modern Arab History (A)
3 credits
Archaeology and Museum Studies Requirements (18 credits)

HIS 310
Introduction to Archaeology and Museum Studies (A)
3 credits
HIS 331
Ancient History and Archaeology of the Near East(A)
3 credits
HIS 361
Material Culture of the Islamic World (A)
3 credits
HIS 362
Management & Organization of Museums (A)
3 credits
HIS 372
Archaeology of the United Arab Emirates and the Arab Gulf States (E,A)
3 credits
HIS 461
Archaeology Field Methods (E,A)
3 credits
Elective: 3 Chr, selected from any history offering. 3 credits
Required Capstone (3 credits) Choose one of the following:

HIS 400
Research Project (E,A)
3 credits
HIS 451
Internship in Museum Studies (E,A)
3 credits
TOTAL: 39 Credits
Course Descriptions
HIS 300: Methodology and Historiography (E,A), 3 credits
This course provides students with skills of methods of historical research; including, writing and analyzing history. It guides students to the methods and techniques of historical research; to reference works and sources; and to bibliography in general. Emphasis is also placed on varieties of secondary sources and the major trends in historical interpretation.
HIS 310: Introduction to Archaeology and Museum Studies (E,A), 3 credits
The course deals with teaching the student how to practice both the archaeological surveying and the archaeological excavation in the field, and how to deal with the archaeological cultural remains in connection with documentation methods, preservation, classification and chronological presentation. The development of archeology as a discipline, ethical concerns and the impact of technology on archeological practice are also emphasized.
HIS 321: World History: Origins to 1500 (E), 3 credits
This Course Provides A survey of the development of world civilization and major cultures form the Neolithic Revolution until the eve of the European conquest of the Americas. The civilizations of the Ancient, Classical and Postclassical periods will be studied, emphasizing interaction between civilizations and major cultures. Topics will include: the origins and role of universal religions; the examination of political, social and gender structures in relation to economic and demographic development, and the diffusion of culture and technology via migration, commerce and the expansion of empires. It should be the first of a two-course sequence, with the addition of History 322.
HIS 322: Modern World History: (E), 3 credits
A survey of the origin and development of the modern world, tracing both regional histories and global interactions. Major themes include: economic aspects of globalization; intellectual and cultural adjustments to modernity; modern imperialism; resistance to empire and the birth of nations; and the historical origins of the contemporary world.
( prereq. His 321)
HIS 331: Ancient History and Archaeology of the Near East: (A), 3 credits
The course focuses on the geographical and historical backgrounds of the Ancient Near East. It studies the civilizations of Egypt, Syria, Mesopotamia and Persia. It briefly deals with the commercial importance, political situation and cultural developments of the Ancient Near East during the period from ancient times to the 3rd century B.C.
HIS 332: Ancient History and Archaeology of the Arabian Peninsula (A), 3 credits
The course studies the geographical and historical backgrounds of the Arabian Peninsula, including its location, and descriptions of its commercial importance and its political situation during the period from the ancient times to the rise of Islam, plus some details about archaeological sites in the Peninsula. It also contains details about the ancient kingdoms, civilizations, and societies of Arabia.
HIS 341: The Rise of Islam (A), 3 credits
The course clarifies the transformation of Arab society as a result of the advent of Islam and the establishment of the first Arab Islamic State. It also deals with the role of the Rashidin Caliphs in cementing the foundations of the Islamic Arab State laid down by the Prophet, in addition to their military, administrative, and financial achievements.
HIS 342: History of the Islamic World: Origins to1500 (A), 3 credits
A survey of the history of the Islamic World from late antiquity and the rise of Islam to the 15th century, with an emphasis on the background and circumstances of the rise of Islam, the creation and development of the Islamic Empire, the expansion of Islam in the medieval period and the rise of dynastic successor states, European encounters and the rise of the Ottoman Empire.
HIS 351: History of Al-Andulas and Al-Maghrib during the Muslim Period (A), 3 credits
The course seeks to study the History of Al-Andulus and Al-Maghrib from the Islamic conquest to the fall of Al-Muhaides state, including the following aspects; Political and cultural aspects during the Wali's period - the Independent states period and their internal and external relationships, and the political and cultural life under the Muravids and Al-Muhaids.
HIS 352: History of the Umayyad and Abbasid States (A), 3 credits
Establishment and expansion of the Islamic state during the Umayyad and Abbasid periods. Emphasis on socio-political changes during the Umayyad period, Islamic conquests and the spread of Islam during the Umayyad period, the rise of the Abbasid Caliphate and cultural changes, the rise of independent states and the disintegration of the Islamic empire.
HIS 361: Material Culture of the Islamic World (A), 3 credits
The course studies the cultural Islamic remains mainly the architecture, arts, coins and pottery etc. starting from the early Islamic Period up to the Ottoman period in the whole Islamic area, and the Arab countries in particular. The study will be focusing on different examples from different historical periods. (Note: if possible, students should be able to examine specimens of coins, pottery and other artifacts in local collections)
HIS 362: Management & Organization of Museums (A), 3 credits
The course focus on the study of Museums its construction, divisions, exhibition halls and ways and means of storing, securing and displaying monuments.
HIS 371: History of Islamic East during the Crusades (A), 3 credits
This course deals with the study of the affairs of Islamic East during the 11th - 13th centuries of European offensive, and its effects on Islamic reaction towards the Crusaders, and the political and cultural effects of the Crusades on the Islamic East.
HIS 372: Archaeology of the United Arab Emirates and the Arab Gulf States (A), 3 credits
The course provides students with sources of the archaeology of the United Arab Emirates and the Gulf countries. It studies the history of the excavations and their outcome. In addition, the course tries to focus on the social, economic and cultural life based on the archaeological cultural remains throughout ages from the Paleolithic through the Late Islamic Period.
( prereq. HIS 310)
HIS 400: Research Project (E,A), 3 credits
This course seeks to train the students in the practical application of scientific research in history. It provides them with the methodology techniques. It also gives each student the opportunity for choosing a topic of his/her own to practice methodology and techniques.
HIS 411: History and Civilization of Medieval Europe(E), 3 credits
This course surveys the History and Civilization of Medieval Europe from 5th to 15th century including the forming of Medieval Europe, the Christian and Germanic legacy, and European institutions in the Middle Ages.
HIS 412: History of the United Arab Emirates (A), 3 credits
This course discusses the Origins of the U.A.E., and its internal relations. It covers the period of the British control, from the early nineteenth century up to the British withdrawal 1971. It also covers the Union negotiations, the birth of the state, and its achievements. This course incorporates the study of a selection of modern documents, written in English dealing with the history of the Arab Gulf and the UAE. It concentrates on translation and analysis of the documents in order to enable the student to understand and deduce the relevant historical information.
HIS 421: Modern and Contemporary Arab History (A), 3 credits
Introduction to the history of Modern Arab History from 1500 to the present. Topics include the growth and decline of Islamic empires, European colonialism, nationalism, modernization, Islamic reform movements, social and economic change in the modern period. Emphasis on the Arab countries and the historical background of contemporary issues such as the Arab-Israeli conflict.
( prereq. HIS 342)
HIS 422: Modern and Contemporary History of Africa (E), 3 credits
The curse discuses the African history during the Modern and Contemporary periods, in order to acquaint the student with the historical developments of Sub-Saharan African Countries from the 15th century to the middle of the 20th century.
HIS 431: Modern and Contemporary European History (E), 3 credits
The course deals with the study of the period from the Renaissance to the last quarter of the 19th century. It deals with the renaissance, its impact, European expansion, the reformation, major developments of the 17th and 18th centuries, the French revolution, Napoleon's Empire to the German unification and the scramble for colonies. It deals also with crises, alliances and Power rivalries since the dawn of 20th century, including the events leading to the two world wars and their impact worldwide, postwar evolution and west Europe, development of Europe in the Past-soviet era.
HIS 432: Modern and Contemporary History of America (E), 3 credits
This course aims at the study of the European discovery of the Americas, the establishment of the early settlements, the emergence and the development of the American states and their relations with the outside world, mainly with Europe. Generally it covers the period between the 15th and 20th centuries.
HIS 440: Special Topics (1) (A), 3 credits
This Course Provides an Intensive study of a central thematic topic or problem in history. May focus on a particular society, but should include recent historical interpretations that offer a comparative perspective. Topics selected by faculty members. (Possible examples: women and the family; oral and local history; migration and immigration; cities and urbanism; environmental history; war its impact on society; connections between world civilizations
HIS 450: Special Topics (2) (E), 3 credits
This Course Provides an Intensive study of a central thematic topic or problem in history. May focus on a particular society, but should include recent historical interpretations that offer a comparative perspective. Topics selected by faculty members. (Possible examples: women and the family; oral and local history, migration and immigration; cities and urbanism; environmental history; war its impact on society; connections between world civilizations)
HIS 451: Internship in Museum Studies. (3), 3 credits
Internship in a museum setting, which should include a project in conservation of artifacts, exhibit display or development of educational materials.
HIS 452: Modern and Contemporary History of Islamic World (E,A), 3 credits
The course studies the Islamic World History in modern and contemporary periods, and relations among Islamic and Arab peoples. It also deals with contemporary conditions and problems of the Islamic World.
HIS 461: Archeological Field Methods (3) (E,A), 3 credits
Advanced study of archeological field methods, emphasizing methods of recording field data including mapping, drawing and photography, practice in the use of field equipment; conservation of artifacts; participation in local site surveys and excavations when feasible.
HIS 462: Modern and Contemporary History of Asia (E), 3 credits
The course deals with the study of the history of Asia during the Modern and Contemporary periods, in order to acquaint the student with the historical developments of the region, with which Arabia has historical, cultural and economic ties.
HIS 471: Modern and Contemporary History of Arabian Gulf (A), 3 credits
This course provides students with the historical origins and developments of the Arab Gulf states. It deals with the social, Political and economic changes in the Arab Gulf states in its modern and contemporary history.
Major in Philosophy
A major in philosophy offers a wide range of opportunities for intellectual growth and self-development to students with a variety of interests. This major has been designed in accordance with the American Philosophical Association's general statement on the basic aims of a philosophy major. For those seeking an understanding of themselves, our cultural heritage, and the important issues concerning life, knowledge, and values, a Philosophy major will result in (a) an enhanced sense of oneself and a fuller understanding of one's place in the world; (b) a keener and more intelligently critical mind for dealing with the problems of everyday life; and (c) a deepened understanding of the basic concepts, methods, and results of the efforts which have made our world what it is today.
The Senior Year Experience includes the opportunity for Philosophy students to work with faculty from professional colleges and practitioners of these professions in a context designed to show them the value of the analytical skills and philosophical perspectives which form the core of the Philosophy major in the workplace.
Programmatic Goals
Graduates will have the following skills.
• An appreciation of the relationships between ideas and cultural development in both the Arabic and Western traditions .
• An understanding of the foundations and history of philosophy.
• The ability to effectively analyze arguments and argument structure.
• The ability to express themselves effectively both orally and in writing in both Arabic and English.
Students must complete areas I — V.
I. Basic Courses (6 Credits)
All students will take the following course:

PHI 310
Ethics
3 credits
Students choose one of the following:

PHI 322
Theory of Knowledge
3 credits
PHI 332
Metaphysics
3 credits
II. Logic and Philosophy of Science (6 Credits)
All students will take the following course:

PHI 361
Symbolic Logic
3 credits
Students choose one of the following:

PHI 321
Arabian Logic (A) *
3 credits
PHI 371
Philosophy of Science
3 credits
* A: Arabic is the Language of instruction.
III. History of Philosophy (12 Credits)
All students will take the following courses:

PHI 340
Greek and Medieval Philosophy
3 credits
PHI 350
Modern Western Philosophy
3 credits
PHI 362
Islamic Philosophy (A)
3 credits
Students choose one of the following:

PHI 432
20th/21st Century Western Philosophy
3 credits
PHI 442
Modern Arabic Thought (A)
3 credits
IV. Advanced Courses (9 Credits)
Students choose three of the following courses, two of which must be at the 400 level and at least one must be taught in English:

PHI 322
Theory of Knowledge (If not taken in I.)
3 credits
PHI 331
Philosophical Problems, variable content; may be repeated for credit (A)
3 credits
PHI 332
Metaphysics (If not taken in I.)
3 credits
PHI 421
Advanced Topics in Ethics
3 credits
PHI 422
Philosophy of Language
3 credits
PHI 431
Aesthetics
3 credits
V. Senior Year Experience (6 Credits)

PHI 411
Senior Capstone, variable content (A)
3 credits
PHI 412
Internship/practicum
3 credits
The Senior Capstone (PHI 411) will he paired with Applied Philosophy. Some possible content areas are given under the course description.
TOTAL: 39 Credits
Course Descriptions
PHI 310: Ethics, 3 credits
The course deals with the main problems of moral Philosophy and its connection with practical life. It covers Philosophical approaches to the nature of right and wrong, moral obligation, the source of moral rights and duties, teleological ethics, moral values. This course is required for the Philosophy major because of the nature of PHI 411 and PHI 412. As the number of Philosophy majors increases, additional tracks may be added, for example. Science, Urban Planning, Computational Sciences . These will not require Ethics as the base. At that point, section I in the major will be as follows.
Students must take two of the following courses.

PHI 310
Ethics
3 credits
PHI 322
Theory of Knowledge
3 credits
PHI 332
Metaphysics
3 credits
PHI 321: Arabian Logic (A), 3 credits
The course presents the main subject areas in traditional formal logic as they were treated by Arab logicians. The course covers the rise of logic as a science, and its transmission to the Arab and Islamic civilization. It covers the treatment of the topics of concepts, propositions, and inferences. The emphasis in this course is one the relationship between Arabian logic and science, and its impact on Arabic science.
PHI 322: Theory of Knowledge, 3 credits
This course examines the various theories of knowledge. Topics covered include the meaning of knowledge, theories of justification, types of knowledge, skepticism, and sources of knowledge.
PHI 331: Philosophical Problems (A), 3 credits
Variable subject matter. An examination of the philosophical dimensions of some topic of current interest; or of the work of some important philosopher. Some samples include.
1. Current moral issues
Introduction to philosophical discussion of social issues such as freedom and equality, war and violence, education, protest, capital punishment, euthanasia, affirmative action, reproductive issues, gender issues, and the state and the individual.
2. Environmental Ethics
Basic theories of environmental ethics, issues in environmental ethics (e.g., wilderness/species preservation, animal rights, pollution control, development vs. preservation) distributive justice in relation to the environment.
3. Philosophy of History
An analysis of the relation between philosophy and history. Students will study different philosophical systems regarding philosophy of history and comparing them with the Islamic concept of history. The course will examine the works of various philosophers and historians from both the Western and Arabic traditions.
4. The Meaning of Life
Does life have a point or is it just a flurry of futile activity? What is it for a life to have meaning? If your life is happy, must it have meaning as well? If it has meaning, must it be happy? How does the fact you will die matter for the way you live? To what degree is the meaning of your life up to you? Readings from Western and Arabic traditions.
PHI 332: Metaphysics, 3 credits
Exploration of issues in metaphysics and Philosophy of mind. Topics include causation, determinism, free will , the nature of consciousness, the nature of being and existence, space and time, identity, universals, the relation between mind and body, and the relation of language to thought and the world.
PHI 340: Greek and Medieval Philosophy, 3 credits
The course aims to introduce students to the study of the main problems of Greek and Arabic Philosophy and its most important Philosophers, and the development of thought from this period through the medieval period. Figures studied will include the Sophists, Socrates, Plato, Aristotle, Aquinas, Augustine, Anselm, and others.
PHI 350: Modern Western Philosophy, 3 credits
This course introduces the thought of Western Philosophers writing from the 17th to 19th centuries. The course covers rationalism (Including Descartes, Leibniz and Spinoza) empiricism (Including Locke, Berkeley and Hume; Kant, and German Idealism.
PHI 361: Symbolic Logic, 3 credits
Elementary techniques of symbolic logic and their application to arguments in natural languages, truth functions, first-order quantification.
PHI 362: Islamic Philosophy (A), 3 credits
This course aims to study the sources, problems and trends of Islamic Philosophy. It deals with the factors that led to the rise of Islamic Philosophy, and its internal and external sources, and the doctrines of Muslim philosophers. It analyzes and criticizes the main problems of Islamic Philosophy: the connection between religion and philosophy, the problem of the problem of the universe and the problems of man.
PHI 371: Philosophy of Science, 3 credits
This course deals with the most important methodological, metaphysical and epistemological problems connected to the rise of science and its development, including the social sciences. It also considers the criteria of demarcation between science and pseudo-science, the problems of contemporary scientific method. It analyzes and criticizes various topics, such as scientific realism, the development of scientific Knowledge, confirmation explanation, laws and theories.
PHI 411: Senior Capstone (A), 3 credits
The content for this course will vary, see below for some samples. The purpose of the course is to build on the knowledge base the student has acquired during the course of study and to see how the basic areas of philosophy all contribute to the overall development of the chosen subject. It must include a senior research paper.
1 .Philosophy and Medicine
This course addresses moral issues arising out of advances in biological knowledge and technology, e.g., concerning behavior modification, genetic engineering, euthanasia, abortion, transplants, rights of patients.
2. Philosophy and Law
This course addresses topics such as the relationship between the law and ethics, law and society, Shari'a and its relationship to "civil" law. This class must be developed in conjunction with one or more faculty from the College of Law and Shari'a and must be co-taught with a Law faculty member. The nature of law, legal obligations, and rights; relationship between law and morality.
3. Professional Ethics
This course addresses moral issues in business justification of market allocation, problem of public goods, duties to consumers and employees, advertising, secrecy, and truth justifications for governmental regulation.
PHI 412: Internship/Practicum, 3 credits
This Course deals with a group of practical philosophical problems in the fields of environment, ethics, and technology. This should consist in a "service learning" and/or internship component appropriate to the content of PHI 411.
PHI 421: Advanced Topics in Ethics, 3 credits
The course builds on the material studied in PHI 310 Ethics to give the student a deeper understanding of current philosophical thinking in the area of Ethics. Many of the topics considered in PHI 310 will be considered in this course, but at a much more sophisticated level as appropriate to a senior year course. Prerequisite PHI 310.
Prerequisite PHI 310.م
PHI 422: Philosophy of Language, 3 credits
Survey of traditional and contemporary problems related to language. Topics may include the nature of language, the relationships between semantic theory and Philosophy of language, how language refers to the world, how thoughts get mental content, the difference between what is said and what is communicated, truth, demonstratives, indexical, and self-reference.
PHI 431: Aesthetics, 3 credits
This course examines the basic themes in aesthetic studies. The course will provide students with specific methods to analyze and evaluate artworks and literature. The course deals with the various definitions of beauty, fine arts, criteria of aesthetic experience, creativity, criticism, and the relation between art, ethics, and society.
PHI 432: 20th/21st Century Western Philosophy (E), 3 credits
This course will provide students with and analysis of the main trends and systems of 20th /21st century Philosophical thought. Topics covered may include analytic Philosophy from frege and Russell to the present, as well as pragmatism, and continental Philosophy including existentialism, structuralism, phenomenology. hermeneutics structuralism and deconstructionist thought.
Prerequisite PHI 350.م
PHI 442: Modern Arabic Thought (A), 3
The course aims to provide students with a comprehensive picture of the various philosophical trends during the 19th and 20th centuries, it will trace the motives, sources and trends that led to the rise in Arabic thought during that period.
COURSES PROVIDED FOR OTHER DEPARTMENTS AND Colleges:
PHIL 120: Principles of Professional Ethics (A) ., 3
Professional ethics is the application of philosophical argument to specific moral problems that arise in different professions, especially business. This course will analyze the fundamental concepts and development of ethical theory and its application in business in order to understand and assess how business does and should affect our lives as well as the role business and its values play in our society.
PHIL 1053: Development of Philosophical Thought (A), 3 credits
This course analyzes and investigates the main trends in the history of philosophy and its relations with social and political conditions throughout history. The course will cover the early systems and main figures in early eastern thought, Greek philosophy, Islamic philosophy, modern and contemporary philosophy.

Language and Communication Division:
Mass communication Major
General Linguistics Major
Applied Linguistics/TESOL Major
Translation Major
French Language Major
Major in Mass Communication
The programs in mass communication strive to equip students with the skills and tools that will allow them to be professional practitioners in the various media fields. Students will be prepared for informational interaction with the rest of the world while maintaining the Islamic and Arabic identity of their society.
Programmatic Goals
Graduates of the programs in mass communication will
• Be competent in both the theoretical and practical aspects of mass communication.
• Have the necessary communication skills in the latest information technologies.
• Be able to demonstrate skill and experience in applied communication research.
• Understand the role of the media in U.A.E. and Islamic society.
• Be able to work in both Arabic and English Media.
Common Core in Mass Communication (15 Credits)

MSC 300
Principles of Writing for the Media
3 credits
MSC 310
Principles of Oral Communication
3 credits
MSC 320
Principles of Visual Communication
3 credits
MSC 370
Communication Theories
3 credits
PUBL 421
Media Law and Ethics (Offered by the Department of Public Law)
3 credits
Mass Communication Concentrations (18 Credits)
Students choose one of four concentrations
Concentration 1: Journalism (18 Credits)

MSC 332
News Writing
3 credits
MSC 400
Communication Research Methods
3 credits
MSC 401
Computer-Assisted Reporting
3 credits
MSC 432
News Reporting
3 credits
MSC 440
News Editing Lab
3 credits
MSC 450
Newspaper and Magazine Production
3 credits
Concentration 2: Public Relations and Advertising (15 Credits Required and 3 Credits Elective)

MSC 342
Writing for Public Relations
3 credits
MSC 400
Communication Research Methods
3 credits
MSC 410
Public Relations and Advertising Principles
3 credits
MSC 452
Public Relations and Advertising Campaigns
3 credits
MSC 462
Designing Media Messages
3 credits
Concentration 3: Radio Broadcasting (15 Credits Required and 3 Credits Elective

MSC 352
Writing for Broadcast
3 credits
MSC 400
Communication Research Methods
3 credits
MSC 420
Radio Production I
3 credits
MSC 460
Radio Production II
3 credits
MSC 472
Broadcast Management
3 credits
Concentration 4: Television Broadcasting (15 Credits Required and 3 Credits Elective)

MSC 352
Writing for Broadcast
3 credits
MSC 400
Communication Research Methods
3 credits
MSC 430
Television Production I
3 credits
MSC 470
Television Production II
3 credits
MSC 472
Broadcast Management
3 credits
Elective Courses:
Students in Public Relations and Advertising, Radio Broadcasting and Television concentrations must take 3 Credits from on the following electives:

MSC 302
The Arab and World Press
3 credits
MSC 305
Introduction to Communication
3 credits
MSC 381
Translation for Communication
3 credits
MSC 391
Communication in Modern Societies
3 credits
MSC 402
Photojournalism
3 credits
MSC 411
Case Studies in Public Relations
3 credits
MSC 412
Public Opinion
3 credits
MSC 422
Organizational Communication
3 credits
Practicum (6 Credits)
All students complete 6 credits of practicum. The practicum can be completed in; campus setting or, preferably, in a professional media setting.

MSC 490
Practicum
6 credits
Capstone Course (3 Credits)

MSC 480
Contemporary issues in Mass Communication
3 credits
TOTAL: 42 CREDITS
Course Descriptions
MSC 300: Principles of Writing for the Media, 3 credits
Instruction and laboratory practice in writing for print and electronic media. Introduction to a variety of formats with applications to the fields of journalism, public relations and advertising, and broadcasting.
MSC 310; Principles of Oral Communication, 3 credits
The basics of oral communication, with special focus on public speaking, interpersonal communication, small group communication, and the principles and contexts of oral communication. The course involves communicating with different audiences (in the UAE) that use both languages in communication.
MSC 320: Principles of Visual Communication, 3 credits
This course looks at perception and cognition, semiotics, aesthetics and historical references in visual messages. It incorporates criticism of contemporary visual images across all mass media.
MSC 332: News Writing, 3 credits
Discussion and laboratory practice in advanced news writing techniques, including various story types and deadline pressure.
MSC 342: Writing for Public Relations, 3 credits
This course focuses on developing students' writing skills for public relations, including press releases, memos and letters, speeches, public service communication and annual reports. "The course provides practice in applying the techniques.
MSC 352: Writing for Broadcast, 3 credits
This course provides practice in the fundamentals of writing for radio and television, including news, scriptwriting, interviews, documentary and magazine formats.
MSC 370: Communication Theories, 3 credits
Communication theories are defined in the context of available empirical and qualitative research findings in this course. Models of interpersonal and mass communication processes will also be discussed. The course reviews and analyzes the "effects" theories and hypotheses, including the "gatekeeper" theory, the "two-step flow of information" theory, the "agenda-setting" theory and the "uses and gratification" theory, more recent theories of communication.
MSC 400: Communication Research Methods, 3 credits
This course involves a discussion of the theoretical and administrative research methods in communication. Methods include the survey, the case study, the historical method, the experimental method, the anthropological method and content analysis, with an emphasis on mass communication research applications. The course also provides for the design of field research questionnaires on a communication topic under the supervision of the instructor, to be followed by class discussion.
MSC 401: Computer-Assisted Reporting, 3 credits
Computer-assisted research and reporting, including use of online sources, the Internet, and spreadsheets and databases to analyze data. Prerequisite: MSC432
MSC 410: Public Relations and Advertising Principles, 3 credits
Topics discussed in this course include the concepts of public relations and advertising, their relationships with other areas of communication, their roles in contemporary organizations. The course includes an examination of theories, and the uses and responsibilities of public relations and advertising in society.
MSC 420: Radio Production 1, 3 credits
Introduction to the fundamentals of radio broadcasting and radio production techniques.
MSC 430: Television Production 1, 3 credits
Introduction to the fundamentals, theories and aesthetics of television production, focusing primarily on studio production techniques.
MSC 432: News Reporting, 3 credits
Development of information-gathering skills for presentation through mass media, with emphasis on interviewing and reporting, developing sources, research and accuracy. The process of reporting from collecting information, interviewing and writing involves in the practical setting the use of both languages.
MSC 440: News Editing Lab, 3 credits
This course, focuses on the Fundamentals of copy editing, headline writing, and newspaper layout. The course provides practice in the skills needed in the electronic newsroom, using the desktop publishing lab facilities.
MSC 450: Newspaper and Magazine Production, 3 credits
This course presents a theoretical introduction to the role of the managing editor and his functions in the Arab press, printing technology, functions, and impact of newspaper and magazine production, headlines and photographs, design and layout of the front page and magazine design. A practical workshop for these skills is offered using manual as well as computer software programs. Prerequisite: MSC440
MSC 452: Public Relations and Advertising Campaigns, 3 credits
Discussion of public relations problems based on case studies. Students will produce public relations and advertising campaigns through work in small groups.
MSC 460: Radio Production II, 3 credits
Advanced course in audio production, with particular emphasis on commercial radio production, including programming, production and promotion. Prerequisite: MSC420
MSC 462: Designing Media messages, 3 credits
Application of effective, targeted visual communication through production of graphics and desktop publishing. The course accounts for both the conceptual and the technical aspects of graphic design.
MSC 470: Television Production II, 3 credits
The focus of this course is producing and directing television programs at the intermediate and advanced level. This course introduces students to field production.
Prerequisite: MSC430
MSC 472: Broadcast Management, 3 credits
This course prepares students for the management aspects of television and radio organizations, including programming, sales, organizational structures, and personnel.
MSC 480: Contemporary Issues in Mass Communication, 3 credits
Major issues and developments affecting journalism, public relations and advertising, radio, TV, and related fields, including social issues, international issues, and issues related to media in the Gulf region.
MSC 490: Practicum, 6 credits
Supervised field experience in a professional setting, on campus or in a communication organization, for students completing their academic preparation. Includes minimum of 300 hours of field work.
PUBL 421: Media Law and Ethics, 3 credits
This course includes the study of the federal law of printing of materials and circulation in comparison to the law of other countries. The course also covers press ethics and conventions, the means of self-review and governmental review, ethics of propaganda, and press reporting. This course is taught by cooperation between Public Law department and Mass Media Department of the College of Humanities and Social Sciences.
Courses in Program that are elective & support other majors and Colleges
MSC 302: The Arab and World Press, 3 credits
This course focuses on the history of the press, the emergence and development of the Arab press, and developments in the world press during the twentieth century.
Prerequisite: MSC 305
MSC 305: Introduction to Communication, 3 credits
This course provides students with a comprehensive view of the science of communication, including interpersonal and mass communication processes and their functions in modern societies. It provides a historical background to the emergence of the press, radio, television, cinema and news agencies, and the development and characteristics of these media. In addition, it gives overview of advertising, public relations, public opinion, and propaganda. The relationship between mass media and Political and social developments is also discussed.
MSC 381: Translation for Communication, 3 credits
This course focuses on journalistic translation from English to Arabic. Extensive exercises are provided for translating simple and complex news material dealing with local, Arab, and international events and issues.
MSC 391: Communication in Modern Societies, 3 credits
This course introduces the concept of communication and mass media to non-mass communication students. It discusses the roles of mass media in the diffusion of knowledge and solving the problems associated with personal and collective uses of modern media outlets.
MSC 402: Photojournalism, 3 credits
This course includes an introduction to photography, the camera and its accessories, lenses, shutters and shutter speeds, printing paper, film varieties, and the darkroom. It also focuses on the picture as a means of non-linear visual communication, characteristics of a good photojournalist, and the press photograph, including its varieties and sources. Practical exercises in photojournalism are provided.
MSC 411: Case Studies in Public Relations, 3 credits
This course deals with selected case studies in public relations in public and private organizations in the United Arab Emirates and in the AGCC states. Students would also be required to -prepare further cases in public relations and discuss them in class in terms of problems and challenges facing PR practitioners.
MSC 412: Public Opinion, 3 credits
This course deals with public opinion, the factors affecting its formation, its measurement techniques, and its interactive nature. Public opinion in international and national issues is presented and discussed. Case studies of political propaganda and psychological warfare are presented.
Prerequisite: MSC 305
MSC 422: Organizational Communication, 3 credits
This course discusses the concept of organizational communication as an important aspect of internal practices at public and private organizations. It focuses on the channels, types, and directions of communication within organizations as well as the parties involved in it.
Prerequisite: MSC 305
Major in General Linguistics
This major is designed to give students a solid foundation in linguistic theory and the relationship between language and society. Students completing this major will be equipped to pursue higher degrees in Linguistics, as well as to enter the work place in areas which rely upon linguistic analysis (these include natural language processing, computational linguistics, and any job which involves interpretation of natural language).
Programmatic Goals
Graduates will have the following skills.
• A good grasp of English language and the ability to speak and write well in English.
• An in-depth understanding of the foundations of linguistics, including syntax, semantics and phonology.
• A deep understanding of the syntax, semantics and phonology of either English or Arabic.
• The techniques needed to conduct research in linguistics.
• The ability to engage in analytical argumentation.
The major consists of two parts. Part I is a sequence of required courses which must be taken in the order specified, culminating in a Senior Capstone. Part II is a series of areas in which the students have the option of choosing the courses which will satisfy the requirements.
I. Sequenced Courses (33 Credits)
A. These courses are designed to give the student a solid foundation in the basic areas of linguistics; the groups of courses build on the material in earlier classes. (15 credits)

LNG 310
Introduction to Linguistics

ENG 310

LNG 320
Introduction to Syntax

B. These courses build on the material mastered in the 300 sequence. (9 credits)

LNG 332

LNG 411

TOTAL: 42 Credits
Course Descriptions
LNG 310: Introduction to Linguistics, 3 credits
An introduction to the nature of human language from the perspective of modern linguistics. Focuses on sounds, words, and sentences through analysis of data from various languages. Students will learn the basic techniques of conducting field study, including a discussion of the ethical dimensions of research on human subjects. Additional topics may include: social and geographic variation, language change through time, first- and second-language acquisition, language and culture.
LNG 320: Introduction to Syntax, 3 credits
A course in the linguistic description of syntax. An analysis of syntax by using modern approaches, this course presupposes earlier instruction in grammar within the scope of introductory language courses. The emphasis in this course will be on English syntax; however, other languages will be considered as well.
Prerequisite: LNG 310
LNG 330: Introduction to Phonology and Morphology, 3 credits
Analysis of speech sounds of the world's languages, with a focus on both their articulatory and acoustic properties. An introduction to phonetic alphabets, including practice in transcribing a variety of language samples. Analysis of the systematic organization of speech sounds, with reference to features and suprasegmentals. The emphasis will be on English, though other languages will be considered as well.
LNG 331: Intermediate Phonology, 3 credits
Building on the material introduced in LNG 330, this course provides students with a more sophisticated treatment of modern approaches to the study of phonology, as well as its relationship to other areas of linguistic inquiry. Students will be introduced to and conduct field study in phonology.
LNG 332: Intermediate Syntax, 3 credits
Building on the material introduced in LNG 320, this course provides a more sophisticated treatment of modern approaches to the study of syntax, as well as its relationship to semantics and phonology. Students will be introduced to and conduct field study in syntax.
LNG 340: Linguistic Structure of English, 3 credits
Introduction to linguistic analysis of English grammatical system, word formation, sound system and syntactic structure.
Prerequisites: LNG 320 and LNG 330
LNG 350: Linguistic Structure of Arabic, 3 credits
Introduction to linguistic analysis of Arabic grammatical system, word formation, sound system and syntactic structure.
LNG 351: Computational Linguistics, 3 credits
(This could be developed in cooperation with the Engineering and/or Maths faculty.) An introduction to the techniques used in computational linguistics with a special focus on the application of these techniques to areas such as natural language processing, cognitive science and voice recognition.
LNG 352: Language and Cognition, 3 credits
(This could be developed in cooperation with the Philosophy faculty.) Introduction to linguistic perspectives and concerns in cognitive science. Topics may include universals of language and the biological basis of language structure, the impact of the computational model of cognition on the study of language and thought, representational systems, theory of meaning and reference.
LNG 401: Language in Society, 3 credits
How speech is affected by age, sex, socioeconomic class, and ethnic, racial, and regional backgrounds. Related learning problems and political/educational implications.
LNG 402: Historical Linguistics, 3 credits
Methods of historical linguistics, language variation, and change. Comparative method and historical reconstruction of protolanguages and protocultures.
LNG 411: Semantics, 3 credits
Survey of semantic theory in linguistics. Central topics include: lexical semantics, the syntax/semantics interface, quantifiers, pronouns, tense and modality.
Prerequisite: LNG 310
LNG 421: Contrastive Linguistics, 3 credits
The course deals with the techniques of contrasting languages, and it applies them to Arabic and English with reference to the various components of grammar in addition to cultural variables of contrast. The findings of the course are applied to the areas of second language learning, translation and other modes of inter-cultural language use.
Prerequisites: LNG 330 and LNG 340
LNG 431: Language and Culture, 3 credits
Ways human language reflects the ways of life and beliefs of its speakers, contrasted with extent of languages influence on culture. The emphasis will be on the English language, however, other languages and cultures will be considered as well.
Prerequisite: LNG 310
LNG 432: English Syntax, 3 credits
A course in the linguistic description of English syntax. An analysis of English syntax by using modern approaches, this course presupposes earlier instruction in grammar within the scope of introductory language courses. The course provides a comprehensive treatment of the sentence in English.
LNG 440: Senior Capstone, 3 credits
"The content for this course will vary, but its purpose is to build on the material covered in the major requirements by focusing on a specific topic in linguistic research and showing how this is related to the other major areas of study in the field. Students will be expected to produce a research paper in which they display both the analytic abilities and skills developed during the course of study, but the field methods will be used in linguistic research.
LNG 442: Arabic Syntax, 3 credits
A course in the linguistic description of Arabic syntax. An analysis of Arabic syntax by using modern approaches, this course presupposes earlier instruction in grammar within the scope of introductory language courses. The course provides a comprehensive treatment of the sentence in Arabic.
Prerequisite: LNG 310
Courses from Other Areas
ENG 310: Writing 1, 3 credits
This course is an introduction to the composition process from the sentence on to the paragraph, with emphasis on editing and revising for format, usage, punctuation, spelling, and capitalization. Sentence patterns and methods of sentence formation to convey a central idea are introduced and employed in the composition of descriptive, comparative and explanatory paragraphs.
ENG 350: Basic Grammar and Usage, 3 credits
This course is designed to activate the beginning student's passive language base and make him/her more conscious of the basic formal workings of grammar in English. Emphasis is on grammar use in oral and written communication.
PHI 422: Philosophy of Language, 3 credits
Survey of traditional and contemporary problems related to language. Topics may include the relationships between semantic theory and philosophy of language, how language refers to the world, how thoughts get mental content, the difference between what is said and what is communicated, truth, demonstratives, indexical, and self-reference.
Major in Applied Linguistics/TESOL
This major is designed to prepare students to teach English to adult learners. It will equip them with an understanding of the structure of English and the most effective techniques for teaching adult learners. Students completing this major will have a good understanding of linguistic theory as well as the practical techniques which allow for the most effective presentation of English language instruction to adult learners.
Programmatic Goals
Graduates will have the following skills.
• A good grasp of the English language and the ability to speak and write well in English.
• An understanding of the foundations of linguistics, including syntax, semantics, and phonology.
• An understanding of English syntax, semantics, and phonology.
• The tools necessary for effective construction of lesson plans and delivery of material to students employing a wide range of pedagogical techniques.
Common Core (18 Credits)

introduction to Linguistics

English Phonetics

pedagogical Structure
