Total: 42 Credits
Course Descriptions
LNG 310: Introduction to Linguistics, 3 credits
An introduction to the nature of human language from the perspective of modern linguistics. Focuses on sounds, words, and sentences through analysis of data from various languages. Additional topics may include: social and geographic variation, language change through time, first- and second-language acquisition, language and culture.
ENG 310: Writing 1, 3 credits
This course is an introduction to the composition process from the sentence on to the paragraph, with emphasis on editing and revising for format, usage, punctuation, spelling, and capitalization. Sentence patterns and methods of sentence formation to convey a central idea are introduced and employed in the composition of descriptive, comparative, and explanatory paragraphs.
TSL 322: English Phonetics, 3 credits
This course studies the sound system of the English language, treating the production and perception of sounds and clarifying the concepts of 'contrast' and 'distribution.' This is accompanied by drills in pronunciation and transcription.
ENG 350: Basic Grammar and Usage, 3 credits
This course is designed to activate the beginning student's passive language base and make him/her more conscious of the basic formal workings of grammar in English. Emphasis is on grammar use in oral and written communication.
LNG 320: Introduction to Syntax, 3 credits
A course in the linguistic description of English syntax. An analysis of English syntax by using modern approaches, this course presupposes earlier instruction in grammar within the scope of introductory language courses. The course provides a comprehensive treatment of the sentence in English.
LNG 330: Introduction to Phonology and Morphology, 3 credits
Analysis of speech sounds of the world's languages, with a focus on both their articulatory and acoustic properties. An introduction to phonetic alphabets, including practice in transcribing a variety of language samples. Analysis of the systematic organization of speech sounds, with reference to features and suprasegmentals.
LNG 411: Semantics, 3 credits
Survey of semantic theory in linguistics. Central topics include: the syntax/semantics interface, quantifiers, pronouns, tense and modality.
Prerequisite: LNG 310
PHI 422: Philosophy of Language, 3 credits
Survey of traditional and contemporary problems related to language. Topics may include the relationships between semantic theory and philosophy of language, how language refers to the world, how thoughts get mental content, the difference between what is said and what is communicated, truth, demonstratives, indexicals, and self-reference
TSL 330: Introduction to English Grammar, 3 credits
A descriptive overview of the forms and function of English grammar structure. With guidance in standard usage. The course is designed to prepare students for Advanced English Grammar.
TSL 340: Pedagogical Structure, 3 credits
This is a new course, designed to introduce students to pedagogical techniques which draw upon the structure of English. This course includes an analysis of a broad range of English phonetic and grammatical structures, as well as models and activities for teaching these structures in the English language classroom.
LNG 401: Language in Society, 3 credits
How speech is affected by age, sex, socioeconomic class, and ethnic, racial, and regional backgrounds. Related learning problems and political/educational implications.
LNG 431: Language and Culture, 3 credits
Ways a human language reflects the ways of life and beliefs of its speakers, contrasted with extent of language's influence on culture. The emphasis will be on the English language, however, other languages and cultures will be considered as well.
TSL 441: Second Language Acquisition and Teaching, 3 credits
Covers issues central to any comprehensive understanding of second language acquisition (L2A): role and nature of optimal input and interaction, role of the L1, nature of inter-language (including variation and learner errors), naturalistic vs. instructed learning, and individual learner factors. Proposed models of L2A are compared with respect to these central issues.
Prerequisite: LNG 310
TSL 450: Skills and Strategies, 3 credits
Students develop skills in using specific second language teaching strategies and techniques for teaching English as a foreign language. Peer teaching is used throughout the course. Focus on practical strategies for teaching the four skills, grammar, and content.
Prerequisite: LNG 310
TSL 460: L2 Methodology, 3 credits
An examination of approaches and methods in second-language teaching, including historical methodologies. A focus on conceptual frameworks for skills-based teaching and learner-centered approaches. Lesson planning and techniques and strategies for teaching the four skills are also covered. Discussion of current assessment procedures. Includes critiqued peer teaching.
Prerequisite: TSL 340
TSL 470: Practicum/internship, 3 credits
Students spend one, semester engaged in supervised work with older children and/or adult learners. This course will build on the material covered in the Methodology courses to provide the students with actual experience in course design, syllabus construction and lesson planning.
Prerequisite: TSL 340
Major in Translation
The Major in Translation responds to the growing need for educated citizens who are fluent in both Arabic and English and who are able to move easily between the two languages - both in writing and in speaking - in a variety of contexts. Students completing this major will have linguistic fluency in two languages and a basic grasp of translation theory.
Programmatic Goals
Graduates will:
• be able to demonstrate written and oral proficiency in both Arabic and English.
• be able to translate written texts from Arabic into English, and from English into Arabic.
• be able to translate orally in the context of classroom, community, and/or business contexts.
Basic Required Courses (9 credits)

ENG 300
Listening and Speaking I
3 credits
ENG 310
Writing 1
3 credits
ENG 350
Basic Grammar and Usage
3 credits
Students entering the Translation Major may be exempted from any of the courses listed above by petitioning to take an exam and passing it at the level expected of graduates of each particular course. Students who do so should make up these credits by taking additional upper level translation courses.
Intermediate Required Courses (12 credits)

ENG 450
Listening and Speaking 2
3 credits
TRS 330
Introduction to Translation
3 credits
TRS 410
Translation of English Texts
3 credits
TRS 420
Translation of Arabic Texts
3 credits

TRS 451
Translation Theory and Technical Texts
3 credits
ENG 312
Writing 2
3 credits
ENG 461
Advanced Writing
3 credits
MSC 321
Translation for Communication
3 credits
TRS 342
Translation in the Community
3 credits
ARB 330
Literary Texts and Text Analysis
3 credits

Introduction to Syntax and Morphology

TRS 472
Modern Media Translation
3 credits

TRS 431
Advanced Written Translation
3 credits
TRS 462
Practicum/Oral
3 credits
Total Credits: 39
Course Descriptions
Existing Courses
ENG 300: Listening and Speaking 1, 3 credits
This course introduces students to four main areas: (1) using correct grammatical structures in spoken English; (2) developing a speaking vocabulary useful for their academic work; (3) standard pronunciation useful for an international setting; and (4) listening and speaking strategies useful for effective communication.
ENG 310: Writing 1, 3 credits
This course is an introduction to the composition process from the sentence onto the paragraph, with emphasis on editing and revising for format, usage, punctuation, spelling and capitalization. Sentence patterns and methods of sentence formation to convey a central idea are introduced and employed in the composition of descriptive, comparative, and explanatory paragraphs.
ENG 312: Writing 2, 3 credits
This course integrates the methods of Writing 1 into more complex writing strategies to give students a solid grounding in writing techniques and skills (analysis, comparison/contrast, cause-effect, argumentation, narrative, description, and critique). It offers classroom analysis and discussion of professional models of expository prose, followed by written exercises in long paragraph writing with some individual attention.
Prerequisite: ENG 310
ENG 350: Basic Grammar and Usage, 3 credits
This course is designed to activate the beginning student's passive language base and make him/her more conscious of the basic formal workings of grammar in English. Emphasis is on grammar use in oral and written communication.
ENG 450: Listening and Speaking 2, 3 credits
A continuation of Listening and Speaking 1, this course includes more intensive practice in communications, most likely cross-cultural. Students will practice communications for academic settings, focusing especially on literature and linguistics as discussion topics, as well as telephone communications, consensus group problem solving, argumentation and debate.
Prerequisite: ENG 300
ENG 461: Advanced Writing, 3 credits
This course emphasizes the techniques and methods essential to writing a thoughtful, carefully written, and well-designed essay. It comprises three main foci in terms of the principal structural states of the unified essay: the beginning, the body, and the conclusion.
Prerequisite: ENG 312
Courses offered to students from this college and other colleges
MSC 321: Translation for Communication, 3 credits
This course focuses on journalistic translation from English to Arabic. Extensive exercises are provided for translating simple and complex news material dealing with local, Arab, and international events and issues.
Modifications of Existing Courses
TRS330: Introduction to Translation, 3 credits
(Formerly ENGL 290)
This course introduces the students to the basic theoretical principles of translation in general and to English-Arabic translation in particular. It also aims at training students to apply these principles to the actual translation process. Attention will be paid to vocabulary, intelligent use of a dictionary, inferring word-meanings from contexts, and the relationship inside a coherent-cohesive text.
TRS 410: Translation of English Texts, 3 credits
(Formerly ENGL 391)
An extension of Introduction to Translation, the course aims to develop students' translating ability by exposing them to other aspects of this skill. These aspects will particularly include the different rhetorical and stylistic means employed to develop themes and unify components of discourse. Topics for translation from English will become more varied and difficult and will include economics, law, international relations, as well as some literary and religious texts.
Prerequisite: TRS 330
TRS 420: Translation of Arabic Texts, 3 credits
(Formerly ENGL 3222)
This course concentrates on improving the students' command of English at the level of the word (increasing their vocabulary), then the sentence (improving their grammar) and finally the paragraph (training them in the components of discourse unity), and longer discourse. Special emphasis will be given to the rhetorical/textual and stylistic differences between English and Arabic.
Prerequisite: TRS 330
TRS 451: Translation Theory and Technical Texts, 3 credits
(Formerly ENGL 412)
An extension of the earlier translation courses, the course aims to upgrade the students' skill in translating other types of topics not dealt with before. It uses both the deductive and inductive approach to help students understand the theory of translation and the different linguistic, literary, and cultural issues related to it.
Prerequisites: TRS 330 and TRS 420
New Courses
TRS 431: Advanced Written Translation, 3 credits
The course is designed to culminate in the student's independent translation of a significant short written work from Arabic into English and from English into Arabic.
TRS 342: Translation in the Community, 3 credits
Emphasizes translating in various community, commercial, and public contexts - such as offices, hospitals, courts - and in situations such as political negotiations and press interviews. The course prepares students for these interpreting situations by developing their ability to understand and analyze a message in the source language and convey it in the target language quickly, clearly, and correctly.
TRS 462: Practicum, 3 credits
Student does an internship in an environment that requires and uses translation skills.
TRS 472: Modern Media Translation, 3 credits
Focuses on translating for popular print media (journalism, advertising) and for non-print media (film, television). Areas covered could include: translation of film scripts and sound tracks; narration and dialogue; subtitling; dubbing; interpreting for newscasts and teleconferences; translation of promotional literature and commercial manuals.
Courses offered to students from this college and other colleges
ARB 310: Introduction to Syntax and Morphology, 3 credits
This course deals with studying the Arabic word structure regarding its derivation, affixation, vocalization, etc.. It studies, as well, the derivative types, the system of the Arabic nominal sentence and its nuclear constituents, and the different types of both the subject and predicate and their relatings; such as anastrophe and the annullers, with a special attention to applications of these issues to the Qur'anic and different literary texts, and analyzing these texts in the light of modern linguistic methods.
ARB 330: Literary Texts Analysis, 3 credits
This course deals with analyzing a variety of literary texts belonging to several genres from different periods. It aims at acquainting the students with the literary and linguistic analyses in the light of the old and modern analytical methods in language and criticism.
Major in French Language
The Mission
The Mission Statement for the French Language Program states that the French Program aims to contribute to the UAEU's role: develop knowledge and culture. It will enrich the Faculty of Humanities and Social Sciences by providing education in a third major language.
This reflects the policy of constant educational development laid out by the UAE University. The study of French will give rise to a center of excellence whose graduates will be able to serve the needs of their country. This policy will enhance the opening of the UAE and its future graduates to a wider world on a truly international dimension. The Graduates of the French program will belong to an elite able to enter with great facility the world of international business and maintain strong international relations between their own and other countries.
Proficiency and fluency in the French language will be the required goal for students in order for them to communicate directly with the vast world of Francophony.
In their country, graduates will be able to forge links between UAE enterprises and their French homologues. By exploiting the possibilities of practical experience in communication in French through French companies, they will be useful to their country and able to fulfill market needs for qualified personnel.
The modern world of international business requires a multisciplinarian approach. Mass communication, diplomacy, tourism and sports are only a few examples of areas in which the ability to communicate directly is of paramount importance.
Students will develop a spirit of initiative and, being at ease in an international context, will encourage bilateral exchange, participating actively in the development of their own country.
Programmatic Goals
Graduates with a Major in French Language will acquire basic language skills and be able to demonstrate a comprehensive written and oral proficiency in French in a wide range of contexts such as public relations, business and education. They will be able to:
• read and understand any text from any source including newspaper articles, literary texts and business reports.
• write letters, reports and other non-specialized texts in fluent French.
• select data and formulate and develop arguments in order to participate in oral debates
• understand any document in standard French (written or audio) intended for inter-personal communication and adequately respond to such documents in writing or speech
Moreover, graduates in French Language will have an in-depth understanding of the foundations of linguistics, including syntax and semantics. They will be able to:
• understand the basic functions of language
• analyze linguistic behaviors and understand how they adapt to different social circumstances
• translate basic texts (letters, reports, etc.) dealing with professional and everyday life from French to Arabic or from French to English
• effectively analyze arguments and argument structure
Graduates will acquire knowledge of cultures and civilizations where the French language is the principal medium of communication. They will
• get to know and understand the wider francophone world
• study topics that address central historical or cultural themes such as oral tradition and cultural heritage, societies and technical progress, transmission of civilization through time, language and communication in a modern society, literature and traditional or contemporary problems related to language.
Required Courses (42 Credits)
Those courses will aim at learning the basic language and acquiring the specific skills to read, write, speak and understand French in authentic situations.

Electives inside the Major (9 Credits chosen among the following courses).م
These courses are divided into three groups. A student must successfully complete 3 credits from each of the Groups. The courses in each group are as follows:
Group A

FCH 331
Stylistics and History of Language
3 credits
FCH 341
Francophone world
3 credits

Lexicology and Semantics
3 credits

3 credits

FCH 432
Applied Linguistics
3 credits

3 credits
Free Elective (3 credits)
Course Descriptions
FCH 270: Language and culture 1, 3 CH
The course is designed for students with no prior knowledge of French. The students will be familiarised with the language and the culture through short dialogues dealing with different topics related to the French daily life, using audiovisual material, thus allowing the acquisition in situation of the basics in vocabulary, grammar, and communication skills.
FCH 272: Language and culture 2, 3 CH
As a continuation of Language and Culture I, the course will give a global approach to the culture through authentic documents and longer texts presenting French culture and Customs, while allowing long-term memorisation, reuse of language fundamentals, as well as implementation of a spoken and written communication competence
Prerequisite: FCH 270
FCH 280: Listening and speaking 1, 3 CH
The course is designed for students with no prior knowledge of French. They will develop a competence in spoken French by teaching efficient listening and speaking strategies and by the basic, correct, and appropriate application of vocabulary, grammar, and pronunciation. The work will consist of comprehension of brief audio documents dealing with daily life and of mastering speech acts related to common communication situations. Students will be taught to speak about topics of daily life so they will be capable of presenting and discussing their opinions in a simple and precise way.
FCH 282: Listening and speaking 2, 3 CH
The raising of a competence in spoken French is carried on with a view to developing greater fluency and the use of varied vocabulary and sophisticated grammar structures. The work will consist of comprehension of authentic audio documents from the media and of mastering spoken communication skills. Students will be trained to be capable of giving an organised spoken presentation as a result of the analysis of a basic written document.
Prerequisite: FCH 280
FCH 301 :Advanced Language and culture I, 3 CH
Texts and documents with larger cultural content will allow the development of students' cultural knowledge and the reinforcement of the grammar, lexical, and communication skills, as well as their basic cultural knowledge.
Prerequisite: FCH 272
FCH 302: Advanced Language and culture 2, 3 CH
Students will acquire cultural skills and the ability to assess critically information and opinions that appear in the press, essays, etc. as well as in literature and the arts. Presentation of the Francophone world and improvement and enrichment of communication skills will continue as in previous courses.
Prerequisite: FCH 301
FCH 303: Advanced Listening and speaking, 3 CH
The goal of the course is to develop the competence in spoken French already gained and to use it in working on different oral forms: debate, report, and synthesis. The work will consist of oral comprehension and expression in interaction (debate) based on authentic audio documents. Students will be encouraged to learn sufficient specialised vocabularies (humanities, sciences) and to master techniques of reportage and synthesis. This will enable them to make a spoken report or synthesis of an authentic written page in a specialised field.
Prerequisite: FCH 282
FCH 321: Reading and writing 1, 3 CH
The course is designed for students with no prior knowledge of French. They will develop a written French competence through the acquisition of efficient reading and writing strategies, as well as the written use of simple, correct, and appropriate vocabulary and grammar. The work will consist of comprehension of brief written documents dealing with daily life and of mastering written communication know-how necessary for writing short texts with proper punctuation, language level, and a given form. Students will be trained to identic intentions and opinions expressed in a written document and to express in writing an attitude or a personal statement over a basic topic of discussion.
FCH 322: Reading and writing 2, 3 CH
The raising of written French competence is carried on with a view to develop a greater fluency in writing and the use of a varied vocabulary and more sophisticated grammar structures. The work will consist of mastering written communication skills to ensure accurate acquisition of information, comprehension, reformulation, and transmission based on brief written documents. Students will also concentrate on written analyses of text content, on how to write brief messages dealing with varied communication situations, to produce coherent text based on notes, and to compose short narrations, descriptions, and formal letters in daily life.
Prerequisite: FCH 321
FCH 331: Stylistics and History of Language, 3CH
This course introduces students to stylistics and its contribution to modern language studies. It will particularly concentrate on selected works written by major and representative authors whose writings epitomise the historical, cultural, and literary milieu in which they were written. Students will read a variety of short literary works as well as different kinds of essays on various topics. Emphasis will be on developing linguistic, critical, and analytic competencies.
FCH 332: Basic Grammar and Usage, 3 CH
Students will receive guided and graded learning of morphosyntaxic regularities, enabling progressive mastering of grammatical micro-systems and methodical acquisition of thematic vocabulary. Grammar theory will be explained through various application exercises. The formal aspects of the language such as spelling, punctuation, phonetics and prosody will also be studied through learning elementary rules and adapted exercises.
The systematic learning of grammar and vocabulary will be carried on though various structural exercises using an approach that focuses on formation mechanisms (derivation, word families).
FCH 334: Lexicology and Semantics, 3 CH
This course introduces students to a variety of texts and genres offered as models and basic texts for analysis. The texts chosen will be selected to instil in the students' minds the basic patterns of French sentences and techniques of writing. Letters, telegrams and electronic messages will be studied and imitated as well as specific texts out of papers, magazines, reports and written debates. This course will be designed to complement the students' proficiency in French and connect them to the world beyond the University. This course particularly aims at illustrating the many links between Language and Communication.
FCH 341: Francophone world, 3 CH
This course is intended to familiarize students with the literary works written by non- French francophone authors. It will underline the numerous and different features of the francophone world and will explore the trends in French speaking literature all over the world. The richness, originality and creativity of this cultural diversity will be demonstrated. The students will be provided with a comprehensive survey of Francophone literature A special emphasis will be given to Francophone writers from the Arab world.
FCH 342: Morphology and Phonology, 3 CH
One part of the course will deal with the phonological analysis of the sound system of French. It is considered as an extension of English Phonetics and aims at making students more conscious of the phenomena of the language, its particular music, rhythm, and intonation. Regular exercises will follow each topic presented with the aim of improving the students' ability in French The second part of the course will deal with word-formation, roots and affixes as well as derivations and will enlarge their knowledge of the language. Finally this course will cover the interrelationship between linguistics and other disciplines such as phonetics or lexicology.
FCH 401: Advanced Reading and writing, 3 CH
The course is targeted in the development of the written French competence already gained and at using it in working on different written forms: text reformulation, text précis and document synthesis. The work consists of comprehension, guided analysis and reformulation of authentic texts, dealing with topics on France and the francophone world (work, studies, transports, institutions, daily life, cultural and artistic life). The course will develop the students' skills in mastering the technique of text précis and synthesis, which will enable them to better express their own views and make comparisons.
Prerequisite: FCH 322
FCH 411: Introduction to Translation, 3CH
After the study of standard French, this course will introduce students to the basic theoretical principles of translation. Attention will be paid to vocabulary, intelligent use of a dictionary, and inferring word-meanings from context The course provides practice for translating and proposes selected texts which provide a variety of subject matter and style, in addition to being relevant to the students' interest and needs.
FCH 412: Communication in a Modern Society, 3 CH
A study of the French language as a social construct. It provides students with a suitable knowledge of language differences, especially with regard to the different registers of French (how speech is affected by age, sex, socioeconomic class and region). It also trains them to adjust their linguistic behaviour to different social circumstances, through the study of the linguistic behaviour of individuals and groups in different situations.
FCH 413: French Phonetics, 3 CH
A study of the sound system of the French language treating the production and perception of sounds. This is accompanied by drills in pronunciation and transcription. Students will be trained to pronounce French sounds correctly by using the modern apparatus available to teach this science.-This course will aim at acquiring standard pronunciation useful for an international setting and will develop listening and speaking strategies useful for effective communication.
FCH 432: Applied Linguistics, 3CH
This course concentrates on improving the students' command of French by studying and analysing a vast range of texts and authentic oral documents. It will include the application of linguistic theory in the fields of group communication, intercultural communication, translation and other modes of intercultural language use.
FCH 442: Translation of texts from and into French, 3 CH
This course is an extension of Introduction to Translation. It will develop students' translating ability by exposing them to a large range of texts. Topics for translation from or into French will become more varied and include economics, business, culture, sociology, etc (literary texts will not be excluded). Special emphasis will be laid on the rhetorical/textual and stylistic differences between French and other languages. After the study of standard French, this course is designed to familiarise students with specific uses of French in different situations. This course will introduce students to the skills and technological competencies that will be required for future work in each area.

Society and Behavior Division:
Psychology Major
Sociology Major
Social Work Major
Human Services and Counseling Major
Major in Psychology
The intent of the psychology major is to provide students with a scientific study of human behavior. Students become familiar with the theoretical foundations of psychology and the methods used to evaluate and assess psychological research and tests.
Programmatic goals
Students graduating with a major in psychology will:
• Be competent consumers and conductors of research
• Understand how psychological theories are developed and assessed.
• Be able to apply an understanding of psychology to real-world social problems.
Required Courses (9 hrs):

STAT 180
Psychological Statistics (1)
3 credits
PSY 300
Introduction to Psychology
3 credits
PSY 310
Research Methods in Psychology
3 credits
Basic processes
Choose three of the following: (9 hrs):

PSY 301
Biopsychology
3 credits
PSY 302
Cognitive Psychology
3 credits
PSY 311
Developmental Psychology
3 credits
PSY 312
Psychology of Learning
3 credits
PSY 321
Sensation and Perception
3 credits
PSY 322
Psychology of Personality
3 credits
PSY 331
Social Psychology
3 credits
(18 hrs)
Choose 6 hours in each of the following areas:
A) Applied Psychology (6 hrs)

PSY 341
Educational Psychology
3 credits
PSY 351
Psychology of Work
3 credits
PSY 431
Psychology and Law
3 credits
PSY 441
Counseling Psychology
3 credits
PSY 451
Clinical Psychology
3 credits
B) Individual Differences (6 hrs)

PSY 332
Psychological Tests and Measurements
3 credits
PSY 342
Abnormal Psychology
3 credits
PSY 352
Adult Development and Aging
3 credits
PSY 441
Psychology of Creativity and Talent
3 credits
PSY 421
Differential Psychology
3 credits
C) Advanced Electives (6 hrs)

STAT 280
Psychological Statistics (2)
3 credits
PSY 461
Neuropsychology
3 credits
PSY 462
Advanced Biopsychology
3 credits
PSY 471
Experimental Psychology
3 credits
PSY 472
Practicum I: Psychological Service in the field of Counseling
3 credits
Capstone requirement: (3 credits)
Students will choose between practicum, internship or research options.

PSY 422
Selected Topics in Psychology
3 credits
PSY 432
Applied Research
3 credits
PSY 482
Practicum II
3 credits
Total: 39 Credits
Course Descriptions
PSY 300: Introduction to Psychology, 3 credits
An overview of psychology: definitions, methods and fields.
Topics: history and development of psychology, research methods, the biological bases of behavior, sensation, perception, learning, memory, mental abilities, emotions, motivation, personality, adjustment, psychological disorders and treatment, and the individual in the group.
PSY 301: Biopsychology, 3 credits
Introduction to the area of psychology focusing on the biological bases of behavior.
Topics: research on sensory processes, eating, drinking behaviors, learning and memory, emotion and mood.
Prerequisite: PSY 300
PSY 302: Cognitive Psychology, 3 credits
The study of the basic cognitive processes and their functioning.
Topics: pattern recognition, attention, consciousness, perception, memory, imagery, knowledge, language, thinking, problem-solving; reasoning; and Artificial intelligence. Students are expected to run selected experiments in specific cognitive processes .
Prerequisite: PSY 300.
PSY 310: Research.م Methods in Psychology, 3 credits
Introduction of the fundamentals of psychological inquiry. The study of research methods used in psychology and the acquisition of skills required to conduct simple research studies.
Topics: scientific method, ethical issues, descriptive methods, correlation research, experimental methods, single-case, quasi-experimental, and program evaluation.
PSY 311: Developmental Psychology, 3 credits
The study of changes in human behavior through the lifespan.
Topics: factors affecting human development, principles of development, aspects of development, bodily, mental, cognitive, social and emotional development during childhood, adolescence, adulthood, and old age.
PSY 312: Psychology of Learning, 3 credits
The study of theories and principles of learning with special emphasis on human learning.
Topics: definition of learning, principles of learning, important factors in learning; theories of learning; classical conditioning, instrumental conditioning, social learning, cognitive processes in learning (e.g., insight), and learning by observation.
Prerequisite: PSY 300
PSY 321: Sensation and Perception, 3 credits
To familiarize students with basic theory and research in sensation and perception. How we get information from the environment, and how do we make sense out of the information we receive.
Topics: five senses(touch, smell, taste, hearing, and vision). Color perception, depth perception, size perception, tactile perception, temperature perception, pain perception, olfactory perception, taste perception. Perceptual deficits and losses, perceptual development and aging.
PSY 322: Psychology of Personality, 3 credits
An introduction to the scientific study of normal personality development.
Topics: nature of personality theory, methods of assessment and research, and major theories.
Prerequisite: PSY 300
PSY 331: Social Psychology, 3 credits
The study of the role of social relationships and social institutions in shaping human behavior.
Topics: social perception and cognition, attitudes, values, social self, positive social behavior (altruism and helping) and interpersonal attraction (friendship, competition and aggression, interpersonal influence, group, leadership, public opinion, rumor).
PSY 332: Psychological Tests and Measurement, 3 credits
Study and training in test selection, administration, interpretation, and construction applications in local populations.
Topics: preparation and adaptation of foreign tests to suit local population, study of test validity and reliability individual and group administration and interpretation of tests in various fields, construction of simple tests.
Prerequisite: STAT 180, PSY 300
PSY 341: Educational Psychology, 3 credits
The study of the principles and concepts of educational psychology.
Topics: psychological principles of teaching and learning, planning and evaluation, student development, settings of class learning, intelligence and individual differences, and motivation in education and applications.
PSY 342: Abnormal Psychology, 3 credits
The study of the symptomatology and etiology of abnormal behavior.
Topics: Normality and abnormality, stress and adjustment, types of abnormal behavior, anxiety, mood, and personality disorders, substance abuse and addiction, depression, schizophrenia and delusions, organic and mental disorders, mental retardation, childhood and adolescence disorders, diagnosis and therapy of behavioral disorders.
Prerequisite: PSY 300
PSY 351: Psychology of Work, 3 credits
The study of the application of psychology in work settings.
Topics: job analysis, personnel selection, performance evaluation, decision making in Organizations, communication, work motivation and job satisfaction.
Prerequisite: PSY 300
PSY 352: Adult Development and Aging, 3 credits
Analysis of the factors affecting adult development and aging.
Topics: physiological, cognitive, sensory, personality and interpersonal changes occurring with age.
PSY 411: Psychology of Creativity and Talent, 3 credits
Creativity and talent: definition and scope.
Topics: definition, stages, characteristics and measurements of creativity; creativity in different work fields; definition and fields of talent; and counseling for creative and talented people.
Prerequisite: PSY 300
PSY 421: Differential Psychology, 3 credits
The study of basic concepts and methods of individual differences, with emphasis on gender differences.
Topics: the nature, characteristics, distribution and determinants of individual normality and abnormality. Theories in clinical psychology; psychoanalysis, behaviorism and differences; group and individual differences in intelligence, achievement, aptitudes, attitudes, motives, and traits; and the role of the psychology of individual differences in educational and vocational fields. Overview and research on gender similarities and differences.
Prerequisite: PSY 300
PSY 422: Selected Topics in Psychology, 3 credits
In-depth study of selected topics in human behavior.
Topics: in-depth study of the selected topic, literature preview, problems in the community that need investigations, report writing and discussion.
Prerequisite: 18 hrs in psychology
PSY 431: Psychology and Law, 3 credits
This course addresses social psychological issues which deal specifically with the legal system. Topics : role of the psychologists in legal institutions, eyewitness identification, and legal decision making.
Prerequisite: PSY 300
PSY 432: Applied Research, 3 credits
Training in the use of scientific methods and conducting research and writing the research report. Topics to be researched are related to issues of interest to the community.
Topics: training in the acquisition of skills necessary to implement scientific research; definition of the research problem, formulating hypotheses, sampling, using appropriate methods in data collection, statistical analysis, and discussion and writing the research report.
Prerequisites: STAT 180; PSY 310; 18 hrs in psychology
PSY441: Counseling Psychology, 3 credits
Study of counseling psychology: theories and methods.
Topics: counseling psychology: definition, scope, and development; human development through the lifespan. Theories: trait-and-factor, behaviorism, client-centered, cognitive; assessment, counseling; fields of counseling especially for special groups, ethical ' standards.
Prerequisite: PSY 300
PSY451: Clinical Psychology, 3 credits
The bio psycho social study of theories and methods in clinical psychology.
Topics: clinical psychology, definition, scope, and development. Concepts and major theories of clinical psychology such as psychodynamic, cognitive, behavioral and humanistic approaches. Students will study psychological assessment, psychotherapy, and acquire clinical report writing.
Prerequisite: PSY 342
PSY 461: Neuropsychology, 3 credits
The study of the relationships between behavior and the nervous system.
Topics: the anatomy of the nervous system with special emphasis on the organs responsible for higher mental functions; the environmental effects on the nervous system; and tests and techniques used in assessing neuropsychological functioning in human beings.
Prerequisite: PSY 301
PSY 462: Advanced Biopsychology, 3 credits
In-depth study of the interaction between behavior and biological variables.
Topics: biological basics of complicated psychological processes such as language, lateralization, sleep and wakefulness, biological rhythms, stress, synapses and drugs and biofeedback.
Prerequisite: PSY 301
PSY 471: Experimental Psychology, 3 credits
The study of the nature and role of experimentation in psychology, designing and conducting experiments.
Topics: the role of experimentation in psychology, basic concepts, the development of experimental methods in psychology, experimental versus nonexperimental methods; training in designing and running experiments, analyzing data and writing the experimental report. Laboratory and field experiments are in the fields of sensation, perception, learning, memory and social behavior.
Prerequisite: STAT 180; PSY 310
PSY 472: Practicum 1: Psychological Service in the Field of Counseling, 3 credits
Training in acquisition of skills needed in psychological service delivery
Topics: training in counseling and clinical interviewing, testing, case study, devising counseling programs. Skills of problem solving, decision making and planning.
Prerequisite: PSY 441
PSY 482: Practicum II, 3 credits
A. Practicum II: Psychological Service in the Clinical Field
Developing the basic skills necessary for providing psychological services in psychological clinics.
Topics; training in interviewing, diagnosing, case study, writing case reports, rehabilitation programs, and working with a team of specialists.
Prerequisite: PSY 451
Courses from Other Areas
STAT180: Psychological Statistics (1), 3 credits
The study of the basic concepts and elementary applications of statistics that are widely utilized by psychologists.
Topics: data description, central tendency measures, variability indicators, and degrees of peakedness and symmetry of data distribution. In addition, normal distribution, standard scores, correlation and their applications in psychology and simple hypothesis testing will be dealt with in this course. Statistical packages will be used throughout the course to work out psychological applications.
Prerequisite: PSY 300
STAT 280: Psychological Statistics (2), 3 credits
The study of the basic concepts of statistical inference and their applications in psychology.
Topics: sampling distributions, point and interval estimation, statistical hypothesis testing, correlation, regression and prediction, analysis of variance and factorial ANOVA. Statistical packages will be used throughout the course to work out psychological applications.
Prerequisite: STAT 180
Courses offered to students from this college and other colleges
PSYC 1002: Psychology and Society, 3 credits
This course introduces students to psychology as a basic and applied science. It includes the following subjects: The application of psychology in education, family, and industrial fields. This course also deals with the role of psychology in management, law, social and military.
PSYC 105: Thinking Skills, 3 credits
This course introduces students to definitions and principles of creative and critical thinking; training in critical thinking, (e.g., concept formation, knowledge reconstruction, and questioning); training in creative thinking techniques, (e.g., brainstorming, creative problem solving).
PSYC 372: The Development of the Pre-Elementary School Child, 3 credits
This course introduces students to child characteristics and problems from birth to 6 years of age. The physical psychological, social, and cognitive aspects of development are covered in this course. It also aims at helping students to acquire the basic skills that enable them to utilize their knowledge in developing the child's potentials and solving his/her developmental problems.
PSYC 4152: Measurement and Evaluation in Education, 3 credits
This course provides students with information about the concepts of measurement and evaluation in education and the relationship between them.
Topics: Characteristics of measurement instruments, the procedures for developing measurement instruments such as tests, questionnaires, rating scales, construction of simple achievement tests, analyses and interpretations test results.
PSYC 418: Evaluation in Early Childhood, 3 credits
This course identifies the characteristics and assessment of infants and toddlers. It deals with the mother-child relationship in terms of acceptance and protection. Behavior rating scale observation methods, and appropriate instruments to assess mental abilities, such as nonverbal tests and drawings, are all discussed. This course builds on what students have learned in the measurement and evaluation course by applying their acquired knowledge toward pre-school children.
PSYC 423: Socialization in childhood and Adolescence, 3 credits
This course explores the socialization process among children and adolescents. Subjects covered are the socialization process, the role of family as a medium in the acquisition of socially appropriate behavior, dimensions of parental behavior, socialization styles, the effects of culture and society on social behavior, and the influence of family dysfunction on the process of socialization.
PSYC 4294: School Mental Health, 3 credits
This course covers topics such as the concept of mental health; normal and abnormal behavior; adjustment; the role of teacher, curriculum and school system in providing and maintaining mental health; teacher selection; psychological services for students; and behavior problems in schools and how to deal with them effectively.
PSYC 433: Learning and Motivation, 3 credits
This course covers topics such as definitions of learning and motivation, skills needed to improve learning and motivation processes; theoretical approaches to learning and motivation and their role in education, educational implications, and strategies and special programs to enhance learning and improve students' motivation.
PSYC 511: Measurement and Evaluation in Education (Teaching Diploma), 3 credits
This course introduces students to the concepts of measurement and evaluation in education and the relationship between them.
Topics: Steps to develop tests are presented with focus on achievement tests. Desired characteristics of measuring instruments and analyses of test results are also introduced.
PSYC 512: Psychology of Arts, 3 credits
This course introduces the main concepts in visual arts within the main theories of perception, such as information, psychoanalytic and Gestalt theories. Samples of painting, drawing, and sculpture have to he analyzed with emphasis upon fundamentals of art and design and their influence upon the viewer.
PSYC 514: Adolescence, 3 credits
The course provides students with basic information about the adolescence stage in terms of physical, psychological, moral, social, educational, learning processes, and problems in school settings.
PSYC 585: The Development of the Elementary School Child, 3 credits
This course introduces students to the characteristics and childhood problems at elementary level in somatic, social, moral, psychological, and cognitive domains. The course also aims at helping students acquire the necessary and basic skills to be utilized in designing educational and learning processes, and problem-solving skills and strategies.
Major in Sociology
Students majoring in Sociology will be prepared with a strong foundation of skills in writing and oral communication, analytical thinking and problem-solving, research, and interpersonal skills. They will have an understanding of the social world, cultural change, human relationships, social policy, and organizational structures. They will be prepared to work in a variety of government agencies, nonprofit organizations, cultural institutions, or businesses. Students who major in Sociology will complete a total of 36 credits in Core courses (18 credits), a concentration (12 credits), an internship (3 credits) and a research project (3 credits). A special topics course may be used to fulfill requirements in a track with the permission of an advisor.
Programmatic Goals
When students graduate, they will be able to:
• think critically and communicate effectively about social issues and social organizations;
• explain and use sociological perspectives to analyze the social world;
• work individually and in groups to accomplish projects;
• use sociological knowledge and skills in career settings.
Required Courses (18 credits)

SOC 300
Introduction to Sociology
3 credits
SOC 310
Social Theories
3 credits
SOC 320
Social Research Methods
3 credits
SOC 330
Social and Cultural Change
3 credits
SOC 340
Social Problems
3 credits
SOC 401
Applied Sociology
3 credits
Concentrations: Choose 12 credits from one concentration, including at least one research methods course ( R )

Folklore (A)

SOC 371
Folklore in UAE Society (A)

SOC 372
Social and Cultural Anthropology

SOC 380
Research Methods in Anthropology and Folklore (R) (A)

HIS 310

HIS 332
Ancient History and Archeology of Arabian Peninsula (A)
