HIS 372
Archeology of UAE and Arab Gulf States (A)
3 credits
Capstone courses: required for all sociology majors (6 hrs)

SOC 430
Internship
3 credits
SOC 440
Research Project
3 credits
Total: 36 Credits
Course Descriptions
SOC 300: Introduction to Sociology, 3 Credits
In this course, students will be provided with the key concepts definition of sociology, the beginnings and development of sociology, and its relationship with other disciplines in the social sciences. The course includes discussion of basic concepts like culture, personality, social reality, social structure, social interaction, social processes, and social institutions. It also deals with other issues like social policy and its relation to social problems and the influence of change and development in generating social problems.
SOC 310: Social Theories, 3 Credits
The course deals with the nature and development of social theory, theory-building, and the classical and modern theoretical contributions of prominent sociologists. It also covers the main contemporary theoretical trends with special reference to the relationship of theory and research.
Prerequisite: SOC 300
SOC 311: Sociology of Development, 3 Credits
This course explores central issues and matters of Third World development. It tackles various theories through examining important works of major thinkers in the field. Students will have the opportunity to study issues of great concern to the Third World in general, and to the Arab world in particular. This course will contribute to the development of students' critical thinking through the comprehensive suggested readings, and effective class discussion and participation.
SOC 312: Urban Sociology, 3 Credits
This course deals with urbanization and the formation of new communities in the world with special reference to Arab and Gulf Arab societies. The course discusses major theories of urbanization, types of urbanization, the urban way of life, and problems related to the urbanization process. The course also discusses other issues relevant to urban planning policies and the growth of modern cities.
SOC 320: Social Research Methods, 3 Credits
This course seeks to identify the scientific approach and the principles of social research. It also attempts to demonstrate the relationship between social research and social theory and the roles of theory in the formulation of hypotheses. The course offers the students the opportunity to practice the steps of social research, data collection, and data analysis using advanced technology.
SOC 321: Sociology of Organization, 3 Credits
This course discusses the beginnings and theoretical perspectives of organization. It provides a survey of important ideas to help in understanding and interpreting organizational problems, especially those related to social relationships in the organizations. The course will study how this affects social reality. Methods connected with the management of development projects as well as social welfare institutions will be also discussed.
Prerequisite: SOC 300
SOC 322: Bedouin and Rural Society, 3 Credits
This course is designed to provide students with the scientific knowledge concerning structural and cultural characteristics of Bedouin and rural communities from a comparative point of view. The course offers topics on rural development and Bedouin resettlement, illustrated by examples from Arab society, the UAE society in particular.
Prerequisite: SOC 300
SOC 330: Social and Cultural Change, 3 Credits
This course deals with change as a human phenomenon. Basic concepts and theories of socio-cultural change are presented. Major changes that have occurred in different aspects of social life, with particular reference to Arab societies, will be examined. The course also focuses on global social changes & its impact on modern society.
SOC 331: Demography, 3 Credits
This course deals with history and development of population theories. It focuses on sources of population data. In addition, it provides the study of population structure (size, composition, and distribution), and population process (fertility, mortality, and migration) including world population problems and population growth trends, especially in the UAE.
SOC 340: Social Problems, 3 Credits
In this course, students will be provided with the definition of social problems, the main theoretical approaches related to studying social problems, and the difficulties of solving them. Selected contemporary - social problems will be studied and analyzed such as drug addiction, crime, population density, computer crimes, internet, and environment. Some social problems facing the Arab Gulf societies in general, and the United Arab Emirates society in particular are also discussed and solutions are suggested.
SOC 341: Industrial Sociology, 3 Credits
In this course, students will be provided with the definition of industrial sociology, its beginnings and development, and its relationship with other social sciences. It also deals with industrial relations, some Industrial social problems, the social structure of industrial organizations, and the relation between industry and society.
SOC 342: Crime and Juvenile Delinquency, 3 Credits
This course studies the development of the phenomena of crime and juvenile delinquency. It analyzes crime and juvenile delinquency, its consequences and motivating factors. It analyzes the changes of criminal styles and juvenile delinquency in the world. In general and in the Arab world in particular with emphasis on the most recent as well as classical theories in criminology. In addition, this course provides skills for planning suitable programs to combat problems related to crime and juvenile delinquency.
Prerequisite: SOC 300
SOC 351: Population and Environment, 3 Credits
The course is designed to study the relationship between population and environment and the impact of this relationship on personal and group behavior. It also studies environmental problems like desertification, environmental pollution, and food shortages. The effect of these problems on population characteristics will be studied. Population increase or decrease and the problem of providing services to meet changing demographics will be studied as well.
SOC 352: Sociology of Education, 3 Credits
The course is concerned with education as a social system. It deals with its history and development, and its relationship to social conditions and social change. It also deals with the organizational nature of educational institutions as well as the economical, social & cultural aspects of education.
Prerequisite: SOC 300
SOC 353: Anthropology, 3 Credits
This course deals with the interactive relationship between the organic features of human being and his social features and his cultural production.
The course discusses subjects that are related to the characteristics of human race, the diversity in human cultures and the impact of the existing communication among people on the present and the future of human race.
SOC 361: Sociology of Aging, 3 Credits
This course focuses on the social and social-psychological aspects of later life. It will examine the stereotypes about and realities of growing old in the UAEU. The course discuses the sociological factors that are associated with treating and abusing elder people, in addition to reviewing methods of how to address Elderly problems. Social policies and social services will be studied. Students will compare aging in other societies.
Prerequisite: SOC 300
SOC 362: Sociology of the Family, 3 Credits
This course explores the formation of the family, marriage, and kinship as a social system. It highlights the transformations that have occurred in the structure of the Arab family in general, and in the Gulf family in particular. It outlines the problems that face the family as an institution as a result of the socio-economic transformations experienced by Arab societies as well as in different foreign societies.
Prerequisite: SOC 300
SOC 364: Folklore (A), 3 Credits
This course deals with the popular heritage as a significant source of popular culture sources in the Arabic-Islamic society in general and UAE society in particular. The course seeks to introduce the concept of popular heritage, its meaning, major fields and its usage in every day life. Illustrations from UAE & various Arabic societies will be introduced.
SOC 371: Folklore in United Arab Emirates Society (A), 3 Credits
This course provides the student with the basic knowledge of folk traditions such as folk literature, material culture, folk performing arts, customs, and traditions. Such topics will be illustrated by examples from United Arab Emirates society, and through studying uses of folk tradition in public life and learn how to implement it in development process in general & human development in particular. It also examines the role played by the state in preserving cultural heritage in UAE society.
SOC 372: Social and Cultural Anthropology, 3 Credits
This course deals with the emergence and development of social and cultural anthropology in terms of its theory, methodology, research topics, and sub-fields. It also looks at its relationship with the humanities and other social sciences. The course also investigates the application of social cultural anthropology in development & modernization. The application of social and cultural anthropology in areas of modernization and development processes are also examined.
SOC 380: Research Method in Anthropology and Folklore (A) (R), 3 Credits
This course provides the student with the main outlines of theories and methods related to the study of anthropology and folklore, including its basic concepts, and methods of collection, documentation, and analysis. The course pays attention to constructing field indicators & its application in UAE society.
SOC 401: Applied Sociology, 3 Credits
This course explores the relationship between theory and practice in sociology. Students will learn about the ways in which sociological perspectives are used in various career fields. Students will be exposed to scenarios of sociological practice in various settings. They will be required to complete class projects.
Prerequisite: SOC 300
SOC 402: Human Development, 3 Credits
This course deals with the fundamental and scientific basics of human development in relation to the dynamic forces in the society. It also concerns itself with the factors affecting human development, and the importance of these factors with regard to labor force policies. The course emphasizes the significance of human capital in society's progress. In addition, the course explains how to enable the labor force to take an active role in development programs.
SOC 410: Assessment of Social Projects (R), 3 Credits
The course deals with concepts, theories, and methods of the assessment and evaluation of social projects with a special emphasis on the following issues: the impact of projects on society and environment, the relevance of projects to societal needs and socio-economic development, the participation of local groups and communities in planning and implementation of social projects, and the common obstacles in the planning and execution of social projects. Students are expected to choose a social project from real life and attempt to evaluate it.
Prerequisite: SOC 320
SOC 412: Migration Studies, 3 Credits
The course is designed to provide students with the concept of migration, the different theories that explain types of migration, and its role in forming the social structure. It further aims at examining the social, economic, and cultural effects of migration in both sending and receiving societies. The course discusses the impacts of migration at the international, Arabic & the Arab Gulf levels.
SOC 421: Political Sociology, 3 Credits
This course presents the political phenomenon from theoretical and analytical points of view. It studies the relationship between political systems and social reality. The course discusses world political systems in the light of the Arabic politics. It also deals with topics such as power structure and contemporary political issues in the Arab world.
Prerequisite: SOC 300
SOC 430: Internship (Capstone), 3 Credits
The internship in sociology is designed to provide an opportunity to apply classroom learning, to practice and enhance skills, to experience professional socialization, and to explore a career. It also serves as a vehicle for the student to become more aware of personal strengths and identify areas in which further growth is needed.
SOC 440: Research Project (Capstone), 3 Credits
This course provides students with the practical training to apply the sociological research methods to the study of social issues and problems. In this course, the student carries out a field study under the supervision of one of the department staff who will guide him/her in formulating, theorizing, and hypothesizing the research question, and collecting and analyzing the data using statistical programs.
Prerequisite: SOC 320
Courses from Other Areas
HIS 310: Introduction to Archeology and Museum Studies (A), 3 Credits
The course aims at teaching the student how to practice both the archaeological surveying and the archaeological excavation in the field, and how to deal with the archaeological cultural remains in connection with documentation methods, preservation, classification and chronological presentation, the development of archeology as a discipline, ethical concerns and the impact of technology on archeological practice are also emphasized.
HIS 332: Ancient History and Archeology of Arabian Peninsula, 3 Credits
The course aims to study the geographical and historical backgrounds of the Arabian Peninsula. It includes descriptions of its commercial importance, details of its archaeological sites, and its political situation during the period from the ancient times to the rise of Islam. It contains details about the ancient kingdoms, civilizations, and societies of Arabia.
HIS 372: Archeology of UAE and Arab Gulf States (A), 3 Credits
The course studies the sources of the archaeology of the United Arab Emirates and the Gulf countries. It studies the history of the excavations and their outcome. In addition, the course tries to focus on the social, economic and cultural life based on the archaeological cultural remains throughout ages from the Paleolithic through the Late Islamic Period.
STAT 2152: Social Statistics (1), 3 Credits
This course aims at providing students with statistical methods for modeling and analyzing social data. It includes data collection, tabulation and graphical presentation, statistical measures, cross-tabulation analysis, fundamentals of hypothesis testing, and principles of survey data analysis using statistical packages.
Major in Social Work
Students who complete a major of 42 credits in Social Work will be prepared to work as professional social workers and/or to continue in masters degree programs in the field. "They will have a strong foundation of skills in writing and oral communication, critical thinking and problem solving, and interpersonal relations as well as the knowledge appropriate for success in the field of social work after graduation. This degree conforms to the Council of Social Work Education accreditation expectations of bachelor degree programs in Social Work.
The mission of the program is to provide a generalist professional social work education that produces graduates committed to social improvement and the advancement of the quality of life of individuals, families, groups, and communities in the UAE and the larger world. The goals of the program are to educate students in the knowledge, values, and skills for competent, entry-level, professional generalist social work practice; prepare students for advanced graduate study; and enhance the quality of life in the UAEU through faculty and student involvement in the community.
Programmatic Goals
When students graduate, they will be able to:
1- use the values and ethics that distinguish social work as a profession;
2- demonstrate liberal arts skills of writing, speaking, critical thinking, application of scientific methods, and promotion of social and economic improvement;
3- apply knowledge and theoretical frameworks that explain the impact of bio-psycho-social variables on individuals in families, groups, organizations, communities, and their networks of interaction, support, and influence;
4- integrate theory and practice in work with individuals, families, groups, organizations, and communities;
5- work with socially diverse populations;
6- understand how social service agencies are organized to deliver programs and services and the various roles they play in the community,
7- know the history of social welfare, existing policy and services, and the means by which social welfare policies can be analyzed and improved;
8- access varied professional development /advanced educational opportunities.
Required Courses (39 credits)

SOW 300
Introduction to Social Welfare
3 credits
SOW 310
Introduction to the Profession of Social Work
3 credits
SOW 320
Social Work Practice 1 (A)
3 credits
SOW 330
Social Work Practice 2 (A)
3 credits
SOW 340
Human Behavior in the Social Environment
3 credits
SOW 341
Social Welfare Policy (A)
3 credits
SOW 342
Administration in Social Work
3 credits
SOW 352
Developing the Profession of Social Work
3 credits
SOW 362
Social Work Research Methods
3 credits
SOW 400
Social Work Practice 3 (A)
3 credits
SOW 410
Applications of Social Work Practice Skills
3 credits
SOW 420
Social Work Practicum 1 and 2 (A)
6 credits
Elective Courses (3 credit hours)
Students can take at least one of the specialized course offerings in Social Work as electives in support of their major. Students are encouraged to choose a course that fits their primary area of interest. Course options include:

SOW 406
School Social Work
3 credits
SOW 421
Medical Social Work
3 credits
SOW 436
Social Work and Social Defense
3 credits
Total: 42 Credits
Course Descriptions
SOW 300: Introduction to Social Welfare, 3 Credits
This course is an introduction to the philosophy, purposes, history, values, sanctions, and organization of social welfare human service programs across different world cultures. Social welfare policy and practice in eastern and Arab countries are highlighted. Emphasis is placed on social welfare needs and services in the U.A.E.
SOW 310: Introduction to the Profession of Social Work, 3 Credits
This course introduces students to the practice of professional social work within the world, region, and UAE. The history of the social work profession, its philosophies, values, and practice models are explored and reviewed. The purposes, roles, knowledge, and skills of the generalist social work practice model are compared with more clinical models of social work practice and are presented as the contemporary practice model for first level professional social work practice.
SOW 320: Social Work Practice I (A), 3 Credits
This course explores the generalist practice model and its knowledge, skills, values, and abilities. Practice skills of problem solving, interviewing, case assessment, and goal planning, intervention, evaluation and feedback are developed in relation to needs of individuals and families. Implications of working with culturally diverse and vulnerable populations are explored.
Prerequisite: SOW 310
SOW 330: Social Work Practice 2 (A), 3 Credits
This course builds on the generalist practice model, roles, and skills, of professional social work practice. Intervention models such as short term counseling, crisis intervention, behavior modification, empowerment and advocacy are demonstrated and applied.
Prerequisite: SOW 320
SOW 340: Human Behavior in the Social Environment, 3 Credits
This course is based on the development phases of the life span. Biological, psychological, sociological or cultural, and environmental (community, economy, and political), factors influencing human behavior are studied. Emphasis is placed on theories that provide a knowledge base for generalist social work practice.
Prerequisite: SOW 310
SOW 341: Social Welfare Policy (A), 3 Credits
This course provides a theoretical model for analysis and evaluation of social welfare policies and the human service programs from which they evolve. Students complete a study of various social welfare policies and the organizations affecting populations and communities in the UAE. Students also learn how to develop proposals for social policies and services that currently are not met through existing human service programs.
Prerequisite: SOW 310
SOW 342: Administration in Social Work, 3 Credits
This course prepares students to take on leadership roles in human service agencies in administration or supervision. Models of supervision and administration are presented through case study.
Prerequisite: SOW 310
SOW 352: Developing the Profession of Social Work, 3 Credits
This course engages students in comparing social work practice in the U.A.E with that of other societies, particularly Arab ones. Issues and needs for development are identified and practical resources are developed to support professional practice at a personal and societal level. Students will develop an electronic database of material about social work practice in the U.A.E. and develop skills in informing others within the society about the profession of social work in the UAE.
Prerequisite: SOW 310
SOW 362: Social Work Research Methods, 3 Credits
This course prepares students to understand and apply research methods and statistical analyses common to social work research. Students formulate research questions, designs, and review research findings. Survey research, experimental and quasi-experimental deigns, field (naturalistic and case study) research, single-subject designs, and correlation methods are applied through case study. Program evaluation and single- subject designs are considered in depth as these are especially relevant to social work practice and administration. Emphasis is placed on developing skills as a consumer of research as well as on developing basic skills in applying research in social work practice.
Prerequisite: SOW 310
SOW 400 Social Work Practice (3) (A), 3 Credits
This course focuses on using generalist practice skills in working with groups, organizations, and communities. Models of therapeutic group intervention are compared to models of tasks or developmental group intervention. Students study community needs and models of community development. Generalist practice skills as applied in community or organizations are applied through case study.
Prerequisite: SOW 330
SOW 406: School Social Work, 3 Credit
This course is intended to explore the background and personality of children in schools. Moreover, it is intended to identify the social relationships between children and their families, friends, and the community. Students are also provided with information dealing with the role of the school social worker in preventing problems before they occur and using different intervention strategies with individual and groups.
Prerequisite: SOW 310
SOW 420: Social Work Practicum (1 and 2) (A) 6 Credits
Through this course students are placed in a human service agency to practice professional social work under educational and clinical supervision. Students compile a portfolio of documentation of their evaluation of their application of basic generalist social work skills in a practice setting. In addition to completing a minimum of 400 clock hours of field-work practice and a portfolio, students participate in an integrative capstone seminar to compare and contrast student fieldwork learning and to build a culture of professional social work.
Prerequisite: 24 hrs in Social Work
SOW 421: Medical Social Work, 3 Credits
This course is aimed at introducing the role of social work in the medical field, precisely, in dealing with patients with physical and mental impairment and aged population.
Prerequisite: SOW 310
SOW 436: Social Work and Social Defense, 3 Credits
This course is aimed at introducing students to social defense concepts and fields, such as juvenile delinquency, prisoners and their families, homeless, and the drug abusers, by using the ecological approach and other theories that concern with the interpretation of criminal behavior.
Prerequisite: SOW 310
Major in Human Services and Counseling
This program is an interdisciplinary major applying an in-depth knowledge of human behaviors to prepare students for entry- level positions in social services and human resources. The program provides students with a sound theoretical framework along with extensive field applications.
Required Courses: (33 credits)

HSC 301
Interpersonal Relations
3 credits
HSC 300
Introduction to Human Services Counseling
3 credits
HSC 320
Diversity Issues in Human Services Counseling
3 credits
PSY 310
Research Methods in Psychology
3 credits
HSC 330
Counseling Skills in Human Services
3 credits
HSC 401
Theories of Individual Counseling
3 credits
HSC 402
Theories of Couple and Family Counseling
3 credits
HSC 404
Ethical and Professional Issues in Human Services
3 credits
HSC 403
Program Development and Evaluation
3 credits
HSC 400
Field Experience in Human Services I
3 credits
HSC 410
Field Experience in Human Services II
3 credits
Theory and Intervention (Choose one of the following - 3 credits)

HSC 306
Marriage and Family Relationships
3 credits
HSC 307
Human Resources/Personnel
3 credits
HSC 408
Addictions
3 credits
HSC 305
Children and Families
3 credits
Electives – (6 credits):

SOC 312
Urban Sociology
3 credits
SOC 310
Introduction to Social Work
3 credits
TRNS 2082
Family Regulations in Islam
3 credits
SOC 362
Sociology of the Family
3 credits
PSY 342
Abnormal Psychology
3 credits
PSY 352
Adult Development and Aging
3 credits
PSY 351
Psychology of Work
3 credits
Total Credit: 42
Course Descriptions
Existing Courses
Courses offered by the Sociology Department
SOC 312: Urban Sociology, 3 credits
This course deals with urbanization, and the formation of new communities in the world with special reference to Arab and Gulf Arab societies. The course discusses major theories of urbanization, types of urbanization, the urban way of life, and problems related to the urbanization process. The course also discusses other issues relevant to urban planning policies and the growth of modern cities
SOC 362: Sociology of the Family, 3 credits
This course explores the formation of the family, marriage, and kinship as a social system. It highlights the transformations that have occurred in the structure of the Arab family in general, and in the Gulf family in particular. It outlines the problems that face the family as an institution as a result of the socio-economic transformations experienced by Arab societies.
Prerequisite: SOC 300
Courses offered by the Psychology Department
PSY 310: Research Methods in Psychology, 3 credits
Introduction of the fundamentals of psychological inquiry. The study of research methods used in psychology and the acquisition of skills required to conduct simple research studies.
PSY 342: Abnormal Psychology, 3 credits
The study of symptomatology and etiology of abnormal behavior.
Topics: Normality and abnormality, stress and adjustment, types of abnormal behavior, abnormal behavior, anxiety, mood, and personality disorders, substance abuse and addiction, depression, schizophrenia and delusions, organic and mental disorders, mental retardation, childhood and adolescence disorders, diagnosis and therapy of behavioral disorders.
Prerequisite PSY 300.
PSY 351: Psychology of Work, 3 credits
Overview of various areas traditionally studied within industrial/organizational psychology. Focus is on issues surrounding employee productivity and well-being.
Prerequisite PSY 300
PSY352: Adult Development and Aging, 3 credits
Analysis of the factors affecting adult development and aging with emphasis on the physiological, cognitive, sensory, personality and interpersonal changes occurring with age.
Courses offered by the Social Work Department
SOC 310: Introduction to the Profession of Social Work, 3 credits
This course introduces students to the practice of professional social work within the world, region, and UAE. The history of the social work profession, its philosophies, values, and practice models are explored and reviewed. The purposes, roles, knowledge, and skills of the generalist social work practice model are compared with more clinical models of social work practice and are presented as the contemporary practice model for first level professional social work practice
Courses offered by the Transactions Department
TRNS 2082: Family Regulations in Islam, 3 credits
This course covers the study of marriage, its importance and the rules governing it. It touches upon such topics as betrothal and its obligations; conditions of the marriage contract; obligations ensuring therefrom; the rights of husband and wife; divorce and its constraints and conditions; the difference between divorce and dissolution of contract; permissibility of divorce; kinds of divorce; divorce by single will, mutual agreement or with the approval of the judge.
New Courses
HSC 300: Introduction to Human Services Counseling, 3 credits
This course introduces students to counseling in families, business, mental health and community agency settings. Emphasis is on professional roles, current trends and legal/ethical issues.
HSC 301: Interpersonal Relations, 3 credits
A course that promotes the development of self-awareness, developing close relationships, resolving interpersonal conflict, promoting psychological health, and a personal examination of goals in one's life.
HSC 306: Marriage and Family Relationships, 3 credits
This course serves as an extension of previous counseling and theories courses with specific applications to marriage and family relationships.
Prerequisites: HSC 330, HSC 401, HSC 402
HSC 307: Human Resources/Personnel, 3 credits
This course serves as an extension of previous counseling and theories courses with specific applications to human resources/personnel.
Prerequisites: HSC 330, HSC 401, HSC 402
HSC 320: Diversity Issues in Human Services Counseling, 3 credits
This course serves as an introduction to multicultural helping. The influence of socio-identities (e.g., race, ethnicity, religion, gender, socio-economic status) on individuals' functioning, concerns, and the counseling process will be explored.
Prerequisite: HSC 300.
HSC 330: Counseling Skills in Human Services, 3 credits
Focus on developing counseling skills with emphasis on self-understanding. Verbal and non-verbal counseling skills are developed through lecture, demonstration, and extensive laboratory practice.
Prerequisite: HSC 300
HSC 400: Field Experience in Human Services 1, 3 credits
The field experience is designed to provide students with supervised work experience in human services agencies. Students will participate in a weekly class that combines the principle of small group dynamics with the acquired skills, knowledge and experience that has been obtained from their field experience. Students will be supervised on site and common issues discussed in class.
Prerequisites: 15 hrs in Human Services and Counseling; HSC 404.
HSC 401: Theories of Individual Counseling, 3 credits
Survey of the major theories in individual counseling.
Prerequisite: HSC 300
HSC 402: Theories of Couple and Family Counseling, 3 credits
Survey of the major theories of couple and family counseling. Also includes process consultation that is used to help businesses resolve interpersonal problems.
Prerequisite: HSC 300
HSC 403: Program Development and Evaluation, 3 credits
Students are introduced to the fundamentals of setting up a program (or agency) and how to evaluate its effectiveness.
Prerequisite: HSC 400
HSC 404: Ethical and Professional Issues in Human Services, 3 credits
A survey of ethical, legal and professional issues facing the human services worker. Designed to teach a process of ethical decision-making and to increase awareness of the complexities in practice.
Prerequisite: HSC 300
HSC 405: Children and Families, 3 credits
This course serves as an extension of previous counseling and theories courses with specific applications to children and families.
Prerequisites: HSC 330, HSC 401, HSC 402
HSC 408: Addictions, 3 credits
This course serves as an extension of previous counseling and theories courses with specific applications to addiction.
Prerequisites: HSC 330, HSC 401, HSC 402
HSC 410: Field Experience in Human Services II, 3 credits
This field experience is to be taken in the final semester of the program and should serve as a capstone experience following the theory and intervention courses. Students will be engaged in supervised work experiences in community service agencies, schools or businesses in addition to participating in bi-weekly 1 hr seminar.
Prerequisite: HSC306, HSC 307, HSC 408 or HSC 405.

Government, Policy, and Urban Studies Division:
Geography Major
Urban Planning Major
Political Science Major
Major in Geography
Geography is the study of the interaction between human societies and their physical environments. Using state-of-the-art computer technology students in geography analyze the spatial distribution of particular natural and human phenomena. Students in this program can choose to focus on either the human-cultural area, the physical- environmental area, or concentrate on the technical side of the discipline.
Compulsory Core Course in Geography (15 Credits)

GEO 300
Physical Geography
3 credits
GEO 310
Human Geography
3 credits
GEO 320
Principles of Cartography
3 credits
GEO 321
GIS (1)
3 credits
GEO 322
World Regional Geography
3 credits
Geography Concentrations (24 Credits)
Students choose one of the following concentrations in which to specialize.م
Concentration 1: Global and Regional Studies *
Required (15 credits);

GEO 331
Economic Geography
3 credits
GEO 400
or
GEO 410
Practicum

Research Seminar in Geography

GEO 432
Geography of the U.A.E. (A)
3 credits
GEO 441
Spatial Analysis

GEO 471
Political Geography

GEO 332
Geography of the Arab World (A)

GEO 400

Current Environmental Issues

GEO 341
Geography of Population
3 credits
GEO 342
Geography of Transportation

GEO 351
Economic Geography

GEO 402
Land Use

GEO 411
Oceanography

GEO 412
Geography of Arid Lands

GEO 431
Natural Hazards

Concentrations: Geoinformatics
Required (15 credits):

GEO 301
Remote Sensing
3 credits
GEO 400
or
GEO 410
Practicum
Or
research experience
3 credits
GEO 420
Cartography (2)
3 credits
GEO 422
GIS2
3 credits
GEO 441
Spatial Analysis
3 credits
Electives (9 credits):

GEO 342
Geography of Transportation
3 credits
GEO 351
Computer maps
3 credits
GEO 382
Geography of Industry
3 credits
GEO 401
Special Topics
3 credits
GEO 402
Land use
3 credits
GEO 451
Digital Imaging Analysis
3 credits
TOTAL: 39 credits
Course Descriptions
GEO 300: Physical Geography (E), 3 credits
The course provides an introduction to physical geography and its relationship to other subjects. Topics covered include the earth, solar systems, lithosphere, hydrosphere, atmosphere, biosphere, and the relationship between these systems and human beings. Includes a one-hour laboratory.
GEO 301: Remote Sensing (E), 3 credits
The course includes discussion about aerial photography, photogrammeric techniques, and application of aerial photography, photo interpretation, and analytical analysis. Introduces students to satellite systems such as LANDSAT, SPOT, ERS-2, et.al,
GEO 310: Human Geography (E), 3 credits
This course focuses on traditional, contemporary, and recent social behavior as factors affecting human geography. Discussion of the human impact on the environment, pollution, population explosion, and the benefits of geography to society are essential parts of the course.
GEO 312: Geomorphology (E), 3 credits
The course covers the principles and recent research on geomorphology. Discussion includes land formation through soil and rock, weathering, chemical and physical weathering, erosion and desertification.
GEO 320: Principles of Cartography (E), 3 credits
The course provides an introduction to the history of cartography, the map as a means of communication, map design/making, map reading, map analysis. Earth-map relationship, map projection, coordinate systems, and data/geoid. The course highlights sources of data for maps and equips students with basic skills such as measurement of profile and cross-section from contour maps.
GEO 321: (GIS) Geographic Information Systems (1) (E), 3 credits
This course exposes students to the principles of GIS (hardware, software, people, data, and methods) and its environmental applications. The course includes hands-on GIS state-of-the-art software
GEO 322: World Regional Geography (E), 3 credits
Introduction to world geography emphasizing a regional approach and comparing characteristics such as ethnic, political, economic, social, and environmental aspects that contribute to the diversity in world regions.
GEO 331: Economic Geography (E), 3 credits
The course emphasizes the role of human beings in investment from economical resources. Key elements of investment such as production, consumption, distribution, and foreign exchange are included. Economical theories such as isolated country, industrial location theory, and geographical models are central to the course. Global economy and current economical problems are also discussed from the geographical point of view.
GEO 332: Geography of the Arab World (A), 3 credits
The course provides an introduction to the geography of the Arab World with particular emphasis on the UAE and the Gulf states. Topics include: natural resources, human resources, and methods used in regional analyses.
GEO 341: Geography of Population (E), 3 credits
The central theme of the course is population and all related aspects such as distribution, composition, demography, explosion, migration, income, disease, projection, and future trends.
GEO 342: Geography of Transportation(E), 3 credits
This course exposes students to the nature of the geography of transport, methods of research on transportation, factors affecting transportation, and types of transportation (land-sea-air). It covers the relationship between transportation, economy, transportation networks, and planning.
GEO 351: Computer Maps (E), 3 credits
The course provides an introduction to traditional methods of map-making and the importance of maps in our daily life. The core of the course emphasizes using computers in map-making and map design, data structure (vector, raster), thematic mapping, and enhancement of communication and visualization.
Prerequisite: GEO 320
GEO 352: Climatology (E), 3 credits
The course focuses on climatology, meteorology, natural characteristics of the atmosphere, solar radiation, temperature, relative humidity, condensation, rainfall, and climatic perturbations. Recent climatic issues such as global warming and ozone depletion are also included.
GEO 371: Geography of Settlements (E), 3 credits
The course covers the definition of village and city, and the geographical location for each. Topics covered in this course include theories of the structure of cities, classification of cities, urban and rural land use, urban and rural population, and urban problems. These topics are discussed in the context of a city and it suburban areas.
GEO 382: Geography of Industry (E), 3 credits
Topics covered in this course include: importance of the geography of industry, relationship between industry, mineral resources, transportation, and urbanization. Theories and rules of industry are also included.
GEO 400: Practicum (E), 3 credits
This course seeks to expose students to real world practices in governmental departments such as municipalities, town planning, telecommunications, police, and environment.
Prerequisite: 15 credits in Geography.
GEO 401: Special Topics (E), 3 credits
The course provides exposure to different geographical topics. One of the selected topics is medical geography, focusing on the environment of disease, health services, and analysis of these factors from a geographical point of view (spatial analysis).
GEO 402: Land Use (E) 3 credits
The course provides an introduction to land use in urban and rural areas, land use theories, and land use types in grazing areas. Special attention is paid to different land uses in urban areas: residential, commercial, industrial, and the pattern of buildings.
Prerequisites: GEO 301 and GEO 321.
GEO 410: Research Seminar in Geography (E), 3 credits
The course covers diverse topics related to research methodology in geography. For example, research methods/approaches, types of research, sources of data for research (geographical, statistical), field survey, geographical techniques for research, steps for producing geographical research, and factors controlling good quality research.
GEO 411: Oceanography (E), 3 credits
The course provides a discussion about the origin of seas and oceans, physical and chemical characteristics of water, the movement of sea water, topography of oceans, biotic life in oceans and seas, the importance of gulfs and canals, and the importance of international sea boundaries from an economical and political point of view.
GEO 412: Geography of Arid Lands (E), 3 credits
The course focuses on studies of arid zones, causes of aridity, topography, climate, soil, vegetation cover, animals, and human life in arid zones. Special attention is paid to activities and problems in arid zones such as agriculture, grazing, water.
Pre-requisite: GEO 312
GEO 420: Cartography 2 (E), 3 credits
This course is designed to expose students m depth to different mapping techniques including contouring and surface creation using geostatistics i.e. The course aims to train students to create and use a wide variety of maps such as climatic maps.
GEO 422: Geographic Information Systems (GIS) 2 (E), 3 credits
The central themes of this course are spatial analysis, statistical analysis, spatial decision support system (SDSS), GIS design and the implementation, customization, and user interface.
Prerequisite: GEO 321.
GEO 431: Natural Hazards (E), 3 credits
Examination of causes, physical processes, and geographic distribution of natural hazards. Discussion of prediction methods and social impact of such disasters.
GEO 432: Geography of the U.A.E. (A), 3 credits
The course provides an introduction to the importance of the UAE geography and its human and natural resources. Topics include analysis of landforms, meteorology, hydrography, urban environment, economical activities (agriculture, industry, commerce), and their impact on the composition and distribution of the population in the UAE.
GEO 438: Regional and Urban Planning (E), 3 credits
This course focuses on the relationship between urban and regional planning and the geography of regions and cities. It lays out a foundation for the planning of cities and regions, requirements for planning, models of planning, geographical dimension of planning, and samples of planning entities from UAE.
GEO 441: Spatial Analysis (E), 3 credits
The course introduces statistical theories, spatial analysis, sampling schemes (clustered, random), statistical tests (normal distribution, X2, t-test), geographical dimension of attribute (thematic and distribution maps), and analysis of correlation between geographical phenomena.
GEO 442: Geography of the New World (E), 3 credits
The course offers exposure to the geography of the New World from its origin, exploration of America (North, South) and Australia, spatial study of the new world, and studies in political entities from the New World such as USA, Canada, Australia, and some countries from Latin America.
GEO 451: Digital Imaging Analysis(E), 3 credits
Resource management techniques for processing digital imagery acquired by land resource satellites.
Prerequisite GEO 351
GEO 461: Geography of Tourism (E), 3 credits
Topics covered in this course include the relationship between the geography of tourism and the geography of economy and other geographical phenomena, global tourism, factors affecting tourism (beaches, archeology, historical sites), local tourism and its impact on development, scenic sites in the world, and tourism in the UAE.
GEO 462: Current Environmental Issues (E), 3 credits
Topics covered in this course include: the natural environment (atmosphere, hydrosphere, lithosphere, biosphere), the environment as interrelated systems, human impact on the environment (urbanization, pollution, global warming), environmental management methods, environmental conservation, and environmental issues related to the UAE and the Gulf.
GEO 471; Political Geography (E), 3 credits
The course introduces the concept of political geography, research methodology in political geography, and areas of its application. In addition the course includes details about "country" as a political entity governed by domestic/local and international relationships, political boundaries, their importance/development and types (land, sea).
Major in Urban Planning
This is a new interdisciplinary and applied degree offering in the College of Humanities and Social Sciences. The program is designed for students interested in working in city planning or administration. It will give students the concepts and tools to design public plans and policies and to administer and evaluate public programs. It will prepare students interested in community and economic development in government as well as in nonprofit organizations. It will also prepare students to pursue greater specialization in graduate programs. The degree combines courses in Geography (GEO), Political Science (PSG), Economics (ECON), Sociology (SOC), Business (BUSA) and Law (PUBL).
Programmatic Goals
When students graduate, they will be able to:
• Understand the principles and practices of city governments;
• Use interdisciplinary perspectives from geography, political science, economics, sociology, business and law to analyze and address urban issues;
• Design public plans and develop public policies;
• Administer and evaluate programs.
Core Courses (21 credits): All students will complete the core courses.

PSG 310
Fundamentals of Political Science
3 credits
GEO 321
Geographic Information Systems (1)
3 credits
PSG 330
Introduction to Public Administration
3 credits
PSG 420
Public Policy
3 credits
GEO 438
Regional and Urban Planning
3 credits
ECON 1052
Fundamentals of Microeconomics
3 credits
ECON 1252
Fundamentals of Macroeconomics
3 credits
Electives: (12 credits)
Students will choose 4 courses as specified below, according to their particular interests in city planning and administration. Students are encouraged to work with a faculty member in determining the most appropriate courses to take.
A. Choose two courses from the following group related to urban issues:

SOC 312
Urban Sociology
3 credits
SOC 330
Social and Cultural Change
3 credits
SOC 340
Social Problems
3 credits
GEO 341
Geography of Population
3 credits
SOC 341
Industrial Sociology
3 credits
SOC 351
Population and Environment
3 credits
GEO 382
Geography of Industry
3 credits
B. Choose one course from tile following group related to urban development:

SOC 311
Sociology of Development
3 credits
ECON 333
Economic Development
3 credits
GEO 402
Land Use
3 credits
GEO 442
Geography of Transportation
3 credits
GEO 461
Geography of Tourism
3 credits
C. Choose one course from the following group related to urban government:

PUBL 2223
Principles of Administrative Law
3 credits
BUSA 2254
Human Resources Management
3 credits
PSG 320
Government and Politics of UAE
3 credits
GEO 331
Economic Geography
3 credits
GEO 452
Governmental Budgeting
3 credits
Research/Evaluation Skills Electives: (3 credits)
Students will choose one course to expand their research and evaluation.م

PSG 340
Methods of Research in Political Science
3 credits
SOC 410
Assessment of Social Projects
3 credits
GEO 410
Research Seminar in Geography
3 credits
GEO 422
Geographic Information Systems (2)
3 credits
Capstone requirement: (3 credits)
Students will choose a practicum or internship, according to which will best fit their career goals.

GEO 400
Practicum 1
3 credits
PSG 405
Internship
3 credits
Total: 39 Cretits
Course Descriptions
Existing Courses
Political Science
PSG 310: Fundamentals of Political Science, 3 credits
The definition of political science and its evolution. It deals with the theories of the rise of the state, the concept of sovereignty, the functions of the state and government and the types of federations. The course includes the study of political parties, pressure groups, and public opinion. The principles of international relations and organizations are discussed as well.
PSG 320: Government and Politics of the UAE, 3 credits
The course analyzes the government and politics of the United Arab Emirates. It concentrates on the constitution and other authorities in the state, including political institutions, the federal political structure and the internal decision making process. Furthermore, the relationship between the three branches of government are studied, as well as the relationship between the federal authority and the local authorities.
PSG 330: Introduction to Public Administration, 3 credits
Public administration is concerned with the management of public programs. This course examines the work of public administrators in different kinds of organizations. It seeks to provide a clear understanding of the political and historical context in which public organizations operate. It also examines the many technical functions such as planning, organizing, budgeting, personnel, decision making process, leadership and evaluation, with which public administrators must be familiar.
PSG 420: Public Policy, 3 credits
The course studies the state's programs and plans as they relate to the process of modernization and development. Issues such as centralization and decentralization, the procedures of allocating the main resources of the state, and the role of bureaucracy in implementing public policies within federal systems will be discussed Examples will be provided from: the United States, Japan, Scandinavia, Latin America and the United Arab Emirates. Prerequisite: PSG 310
PSG 452: Governmental Budgeting, 3 credits
The course introduces public budgeting and finance and demonstrates its role as an instrument in managing development and economic stability. It also explains managerial functions provided by the management of public budgeting and finance in the area of planning, follow-up and evaluation, with the presentation of budgeting organization steps-from classical methods to modern ones, such as P.P .B.S., performance budgeting and zero base budgeting.
Prerequisite: PSG 330
Geography
GEO 331: Economic Geography, 3 credits
The course emphasizes the role of human beings in investment from economical resources. Key elements of investment such as production, consumption, distribution, and foreign exchange are included. Economical theories such as isolated country, industrial location theory, and geographical models are central to the course. Global economy and current economical problems are also discussed from the geographical point of view.
GEO 341: Geography of Population, 3 credits
The central theme of the course is population and all related aspects such as distribution, composition, demography, explosion, migration, income, disease, projection, and future trends.
GEO 382: Geography of Industry, 3 credits
Topics covered in this course include: importance of the geography of industry, relationship between industry, mineral resources, transportation, and urbanization. Theories and rules of industry are also included.
GEO 402: Land Use, 3 credits
The course provides an introduction to land use in urban and rural areas, land use theories, and land use types in grazing areas. Special attention is paid to different land uses in urban areas: residential, commercial, industrial, and the pattern of buildings. Remote sensing and GIS are used as tools to help in land use analysis.
GEO 422: Geographic Information Systems (2), 3 credits
The central themes of this course are spatial analysis, statistical analysis, spatial decision support system (SDSS), GIS design and implementation, customization, and user interface.
Prerequisite: GEO 321
GEO 442: Geography of Transportation, 3 credits
This course exposes students to the nature of the geography of transport, methods of research on transportation, factors affecting transportation, and types of transportation (land-sea-air). It covers the relationship between transportation; economy; transportation networks, and planning.
GEO 461: Geography of Tourism, 3 credits
Topics covered in this course include the relationship between the geography of tourism and the geography of economy and other geographical phenomena, global tourism, factors affecting tourism (beaches, archeology, historical sites), local tourism and its impact an development, scenic sites in the world, and tourism in the UAE.
Economics
ECON 333: Economic Development, 3 credits
Basic concepts and theories of economic development. The course includes economic characteristics of developing countries, strategies of development, human development, the financing of economic development, globalization, environment, and major development issues in the UAE and the Arab world.
Prerequisite: ECON 1252
ECON 1052: Fundamentals of Microeconomics, 3 credits
Basic economic concepts, theories and tools of microeconomic analysis. Topics include demand and supply analysis, various market structures and their equilibria, and the market for factors of production. The course uses these basic economic tools to analyze diverse problems.
ECON 1252: Fundamentals of Macroeconomics, 3 credits
Basic concepts, principles, and tools of macroeconomic analysis, national income analysis, unemployment, inflation, money and banking, economic growth, and government economic policy, international finance and international trade.
Prerequisite: ECON 1052 or AGRB 200
Public Law
PUBL 2223: Principles of Administrative Law, 3 credits
The course covers the definition of administrative law, its sources, the activities of the public administration (i.e., execution of laws, preservation of public order, and public utilities). The legal means of public administration; administrative decisions and administrative contracts are also within the scope of this course.
Modified Courses
Political Science
PSG 340: Methods of Research in Political Science, 3 credits
This course introduces students to the logic and practice of systematic research in political science. Topics include the philosophy of social science, research design, measurement principles, probability sampling, data collection methods, data analysis, hypothesis testing, and data presentation.
Prerequisite: PSG310
PSG 405: Internship, 3 credits
Students are placed an appropriate institution where they can apply their curricular knowledge in a practical setting. The student should spend a minimum of 150 hours at the internship site. The student should submit an analytical paper based on the internship.
Prerequisite: Completed at least 87 semester hours, including at least 21 hours in the political science curriculum
Geography
GEO 321 :(GIS) Geographic Information Systems (1), 3 credits
The objective of the GIS course is to expose students to the principles of GIS (hardware, software, people, data, and methods) and its environmental applications. The course includes hands-on GIS state-of-the-art software (deleted prerequisite]
GEO 400: Practicum (1), 3 credits
The objective of training is to expose students to the real world practices in governmental departments such as municipalities, town planning, telecommunications, police, and environment.
Prerequisite: 15 hrs in Geography or Urban planning.
GEO 410: Research Seminar in Geography, 3 credits
The Course covers diverse topics related to research methodology in geography. For example, research methods/approaches, types of research, sources of data for research (geographical, statistical), field survey, geographical techniques for research, steps for producing geographical research, and factors controlling good quality research, [deleted prerequisite]
GEO 438: Regional and Urban Planning, 3 credits
This course focuses on the relationship between urban and regional planning, the geography of regions and cities. It lays out a foundation for the planning of cities and regions, requirements for planning, models of planning, geographical dimension of planning, and samples of planning entities frorn UAE. [delete prerequisite]
Sociology
SOC 311: Sociology of Development (E), 3 credits
This course explores central issues and matters of Third World development. It tackles various theories through examining important works of major thinkers in the field. Students will have the opportunity to study issues of great concern to the Third World in general, and to the Arab world in particular. This course will contribute to the development of students' critical thinking through the comprehensive suggested readings, and effective class discussion and participation.. [deleted prerequisite]
SOC 312: Urban Sociology, 3 credits
This course deals with urbanization and the formation of new communities in the world with special reference to Arab and Gulf Arab societies. The course discusses major theories of urbanization, types of urbanization, the urban way of life, and problems related to the urbanization process. The course also discusses other issues relevant to urban planning policies and the growth of modern cities, [deleted prerequisite]
SOC 330: Social and Cultural Change, 3 credits
This course deals with change as a human phenomenon. Basic concepts and theories of socio-cultural change are presented. Major changes that have occurred in different aspects of social life, with particular reference to Arab societies, will be examined. [deleted prerequisite]
SOC 340: Social Problems, 3 credits
In this course, students will be provided with the definition of social problems, the main theoretical approaches related to studying social problems, and the difficulties of solving them. Selected contemporary - social problems will be studied and analyzed such as drug addiction, crime, population density, and environment. Some social problems facing the Arab Gulf societies in general, and the United Arab Emirates society in particular are also discussed and solutions are suggested, [deleted prerequisite]
SOC 341: Industrial Sociology, 3 credits
In this course, students will be provided with the definition of industrial sociology, its beginnings and development, and its relationship with other social sciences. It also deals with industrial relations, some Industrial social problems, the social structure of industrial organizations, and the relation between industry and society, [deleted prerequisite]
SOC 351: Population and Environment, 3 credits
This course is designed to study the relationship between population and environment and the impact of this relationship on personal and group behavior. It also studies environmental problems like desertification, environmental pollution, and food shortages. The effect of these problems on population characteristics will be studied. Population increase or decrease and the problem of providing services to meet changing demographics will be studied as well. [deleted prerequisite]
SOC 410: Assessment of Social Projects, 3 credits
The course deals with concepts, theories, and methods of the assessment and evaluation of social projects, with a special emphasis on the following issues: the impact of projects on society and environment, the relevance of projects to societal needs and socio-economic development, the participation, of local groups and communities in planning and implementation of social projects, and the common obstacles in the planning and execution of social projects.
Prerequisite: SOCI 230 [change prerequisite]
Business
BUSA 2254: Human Resources Management, 3 credits
This course aims to introduce students to the nature of human resources management in business organizations. This includes studying job descriptions, analysis, design, selection, appointment, training, payment, and evaluation.
Prerequisite: BUSA 1053 or PSG 330
Major in Political Science
The Political Science major is designed to give students an understanding of the ideas, processes, and institutions that structure political and governmental action in the modern world. Students will explore the methodologies that guide contemporary analysis of politics and governments, and learn about how decision-making works at a local and international level.
After completing the required courses (18 credits), students may choose one of two concentrations for the Political Science major: International Politics and Political Systems or National/Local Politics, Government, and Administration (18 credits). Two courses (6 credits) are required as a capstone experience for the major. The total number of credits for the major is 42.
Required Courses (18 credits)

PSG 310

Course Descriptions
PSG 310: Fundamentals of Political Science, 3 credits
The definition of political science and its evolution. The course deals with the theories of the rise of the state, the concept of sovereignty, the functions of the state and government and the types of federations. The course includes the study of political parties, pressure groups, and public opinion. The principles of international relations and organizations are discussed as well.
PSG 320: Government and Politics of the UAE (Arabic), 3 credits
The course analyzes the government and politics of the United Arab Emirates. It concentrates on the constitution and other authorities in the state, including political institutions, the federal political structure and the internal decision making process. Furthermore, the relationships among the three branches of government are studied, as well as the relationship between the federal authority and the local authorities.
PSG 330: Introduction to Public Administration (Arabic), 3 credits
Public administration is concerned with the management of public programs. This course examines the work of public administrators in different kinds of organizations. It seeks to provide a clear understanding of the political and historical context in which public organizations operate. It also examines the many technical functions such as planning, organizing, budgeting, personnel, decision making process, leadership and evaluation, with which public administrators must be familiar.
PSG 340: Methods of Research in Political Science, 3 credits
This course introduces students to the logic and practice of systematic research within political science. Topics include the philosophy of social science, research design, measurement principles, probability sampling, data collection methods, data analysis, hypothesis testing, and data presentation.
Prerequisite: PSG 310.
PSG 350: Principles of International Relations, 3 credits
The study of approaches, theories, and principles of international relations. The course focuses mainly on different models of international relations and its development, explaining the different factors that influence and shape the development and character of each model. The course deals with the political interactions of world political actors, and places emphasis on the quantitative method in international relations.
Prerequisite: PSG 310
PSG 360: Political Thought, 3 credits
The course covers examples of Western political thought from ancient times to the present. It also deals with the evolution of modern political thought through the study of some basic philosophical concepts such as natural law, the social contract, individualism, liberalism, conservatism, and socialism, etc.
Prerequisite PSG 310
PSG 370: Comparative Political Systems, 3 credits
The course introduces a comprehensive theoretical and comparative basis for the study of contemporary political systems. This includes an analysis of the specific nature and the salient characteristics of each system, as well as the main variables that influence its evolution. The range of comparative analysis extends to include Western, Eastern, and Third World countries.
Prerequisite PSG 310
PSG 405: Internship, 3 credits
Students are placed an appropriate institution where they can apply their curricular knowledge in a practical setting. The student should spend a minimum of 150 hours at the internship site. The student should submit an analytical paper based on the internship.
Prerequisite: Completed 21 hours in the political science curriculum.
PSG 410: International Political Economy, 3 credits
The politics of international trade, finance, and investment including the associated institutions and processes.
Prerequisite: PSG 350
PSG 411: Diplomatic Systems, 3 credits
The course analyzes the general basis and the roles that guide and control the close relationship between foreign policy and diplomacy. Studying diplomacy is accomplished in accordance with legal procedures, diplomatic and strategic objectives of the state system. The course focuses on studying the theoretical as well as the practical dimensions of diplomacy.
Prerequisite: PSG 350
PSG 412: International Organizations, 3 credits
Analytical study of international and regional organizations, examining their history, charters, infrastructure, and operations, focusing on the United Nations, the Arab League, and the Gulf Cooperation Council. The influential role that international organizations play in consolidating interdependence and integration among nations under the umbrella of the new world order is also discussed and evaluate.
Prerequisite: PSG 350
PSG 420: Public Policy, 3 credits
Studying the state's programs and plans as they relate to the process of modernization and development. Issues such as centralization and decentralization, the procedures of allocating the main resources of the state, and the role of bureaucracy in implementing public policies within federal systems will be discussed. Examples will be provide from: the Unites States, Japan, Scandinavia, Latin America and the United Arab Emirates.
Prerequisite PSG 310.
PSG 421: Foreign Policy of the Arab States (Arabic), 3 credits
The analysis of political, economic, social, and constitutional factors that influence the making and implementation of the foreign policies of a number of Arab states. Emphasis is given to the comparative analysis of patterns of interactions, functions, and performance. Some current vital issues such as: Arabs and the new international order, the Arab regional system since the eruption of the second Gulf crisis, the evolution of the peace process in the Middle East, national quarrels over the division of water resources in the region are all introduced and analyzed.
Prerequisite: PSG 350
PSG 422: Foreign Policy of the Great Powers, 3 credits
A survey of the basic principles and factors that affect the process of making the foreign policies of the great powers, with emphasis on their objectives and dimensions within the context of a rapidly changing world. Special attention is given to the analysis of bilateral relations between each great power and developing countries, particularly Arab and Islamic states.
Prerequisite: PSG 350
PSG 430: Special Topics, 3 credits
The course deals with topics in the fields of specialization that are not ordinarily offered through courses in the curriculum. It is intended to provide students with the most up-to-date and latest developments in the discipline of Political Science, on the one hand, and make use of the various fields of specialization, knowledge, experiences, and interests of the department staff and visiting scholars, on the other hand.
Prerequisite: PSG 350 or PSG 370
PSG 432: Europe and the United States, 3 credits
Deferent aspect of European and American politics. It analyzes historical and contemporary trends pertaining to evolution of the political institutions and processes in Western democracies. European integration is dealt with through studying the political institutions and processes of the European Union. The interactions between the domestic politics of the EU member-states and the United States.
Prerequisite: PSG 370
PSG 441: Gulf and Arabian Peninsula Affairs (Arabic), 3 credits
Study of the geopolitical and economic importance of the Arabian Gulf region and Arabian Peninsula. It focuses on the analysis of the behavior of the states of the region towards those regional and national issues that affect their national interests. Special emphasis is placed on security considerations. Attention is also given to the role of oil, challenges of development, expatriate labor force, and prospects of integration.
Prerequisite PSG 370.
PSG 451: Local Government and Local Administration (Arabic), 3 credits
This course covers the concept of local government and its relations with local administration, its foundation and development in old and modern societies and its function in districts and regions, the comparison of different local government and administration systems with emphasis on the U.A.E., the management of cities and city councils, the political, economic, and legal relations between local and central governments, the positive and negative aspects of local government, its effects on the role of local administration, and its capabilities in serving society.
Prerequisite: PSG 330.
PSG 452: Governmental Budgeting (Arabic), 3 credits
The course introduces public budgeting and finance and demonstrates its role as an instrument in managing development and economic stability. It also explains managerial functions provided by the management of public budgeting and finance in the area of planning, follow-up and evaluation, with the presentation of budgeting organization steps from classical methods to modern ones, such as P.P.B.S., performance budgeting and zero base budgeting.
Prerequisite: PSG 330
Courses from Other Areas
PUBL 2223: Principles of Administrative Law, 3 credits
The course covers the definition of administrative law, its sources, the activities of the public administration (i.e., execution of laws, preservation of public order, and public utilities). "The legal means of public administration; administrative decisions and administrative contracts are also within the scope of the course.
PUBL 3373: Public International Law, 3 credits
This course includes definition of Public International Law, its sources and subjects. It studies the relations between domestic law and public international law, public international law in times of peace, international treaties, establishment of states, their rights and obligations, settlement of international disputes, and the law of war, with a focus on duties of occupying states and prisoners of war.
ECON 1052: Fundamentals of Microeconomics, 3 credits
Basic economic concepts, theories and tools of microeconomic analysis. Topics include demand and supply analysis, various market structures and their equilibrium, and the market for factors of production. This course uses these basic economic tools to analyze diverse problems.
ECON 1252: Fundamentals of Macroeconomics, 3 credits
Basic concepts, principles and tools of macroeconomic analysis, national income analysis, unemployment, inflation, money and banking, economic growth, and government economic policy, international finance and international trade.
SOC 421: Political Sociology, 3 credits
This course presents the political phenomenon from theoretical and analytical points of view. It studies the relationship between political systems and social reality. It also deals with topics such as power, structure, and contemporary political issues in the Arab World.
MSC 412: Public Opinion, 3 credits
This course deals with public opinion, the factors affecting its formation, its measurement techniques, and its interactive nature. Public opinion in international and national issues is presented and discussed. Case studies of political propaganda and psychological warfare are presented
BUSA 2254: Human Resources Management, 3 credits
This course aims to introduce students to the nature of human resources management in business organizations. This includes studying the differences in job descriptions, analysis, design, evaluation, selection, appointment, training, payment, and evaluation.
Prerequisite: PSG330
